full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the Alaska Division of Elections, opened in 2024.

In a June 2024 findings letter, DOJ documented that Alaska's polling places — including those used for state and federal elections — have widespread accessibility problems. Wheelchair users can't reach entrances. Blind voters can't use the equipment provided. Polling places lack accessible parking, accessible paths of travel, ramps, or accessible voting booths. The state's ballot delivery systems for voters with print disabilities don't meet ADA Title II and Help America Vote Act (HAVA) standards.

DOJ is now in active investigation phase. Unlike a settled consent decree, this case has NOT yet been resolved — the federal government is gathering evidence, may file a lawsuit if Alaska doesn't come to terms, and is actively seeking accounts from affected voters.

If you have a disability and you've had trouble voting in Alaska, your experience can support DOJ's investigation. You can file a complaint directly or contact the federal investigators on the case. Stories from real voters drive the case.

Voters with disabilities who encountered barriers should report:
- Polling-place physical barriers (entrance, parking, paths of travel).
- Inaccessible voting equipment (no accessible machine, machine broken, untrained poll workers).
- Mail-ballot inaccessibility (paper-only, no accessible electronic option for print-disabled voters).
- Curbside voting refusals.
- Any disability-related denial of accommodation.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division, in coordination with the Voting Section, opened an investigation of the Alaska Division of Elections in 2024 under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq.; the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081; and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794.

A June 2024 findings letter documents systemic deficiencies: polling-place physical access failures under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and ADA Standards (entrances, parking, paths of travel, accessible voting booths); accessible-voting-equipment deployment and operational failures (BMD/accessible-equipment availability, functionality, and poll-worker training); inaccessible ballot delivery to print-disabled voters; inadequate curbside voting alternatives.

The investigation remains in active phase — not yet resolved by consent decree or litigation. Voters with disabilities encountering barriers should report contemporaneously to support the evidence record. DOJ may pursue formal litigation under § 12133 absent voluntary compliance.

Reports should be filed with: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (ADA.gov; voting.section@usdoj.gov for civil-rights-specific intake); Alaska Division of Elections (administrative record); Disability Law Center of Alaska (state Protection & Advocacy entity); Alaska Native Civil Rights organizations where applicable for tribal-affiliated voters.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Alaska right now. The case is not over yet — they're still gathering information.

DOJ said in 2024 that Alaska has serious problems with voting. Many polling places have no wheelchair access. The voting machines don't work for blind voters. Mail ballots come on paper that print-disabled voters can't read on their own.

You can help. If you have a disability and you've had trouble voting in Alaska — at a polling place, voting by mail, or in any way — tell DOJ. They use real stories from voters to build their case.

You can:
- File a free complaint at ADA.gov.
- Tell the Disability Law Center of Alaska.
- File a complaint with the Alaska Division of Elections.

What to report:
- Polling places you couldn't enter.
- Voting machines that didn't work for you.
- Mail ballots you couldn't fill out on your own.
- Times you were refused curbside voting.
- Any time someone said you couldn't get an accommodation.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement litigation under 42 U.S.C. § 12133
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without contemporaneous documentation. DOJ has independent investigative authority and can subpoena Alaska records. Reporting channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (ADA.gov); Disability Law Center of Alaska; Alaska Division of Elections; tribal advocacy entities for Alaska Native voters.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can share your story without records:
- File a free complaint at ADA.gov.
- Call DOJ's Disability Rights Section: 800-514-0301.
- Contact the Disability Law Center of Alaska.
- File with the Alaska Division of Elections to create an administrative record.
- Tribal members can also work through tribal advocacy organizations.
short_description: DOJ findings letter alleges Alaska's polling places and ballot systems are inaccessible.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II, HAVA, and Section 504 of Alaska Division of Elections. June 2024 findings letter alleges systemic polling-place accessibility deficiencies and inaccessible ballot/voting-system infrastructure. Investigation in active evidence-gathering phase.
short_description_simple: The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Alaska right now. DOJ says many polling places aren't accessible and the voting system doesn't work for people with disabilities. If you've had trouble voting in Alaska, share your story — it helps the investigation.

NOT covered:

questions.prompt: Are you (or were you) a parent in Arizona?
Do you have any disability — physical, mental health, intellectual, sensory, cognitive, or other?
Has Arizona DCS been involved with your family? (Open investigation, removal, court hearings, service plan, reunification, termination of parental rights)
Do you think DCS treated you unfairly because of your disability, OR didn't give you the help you needed to parent?
Did you ever ask DCS for an accommodation (interpreter, accessible documents, etc.)? What happened?
Is the case still ongoing or closed? Where are your children now?
questions.purpose: Urgency routing; cases still ongoing may benefit from immediate legal help.

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Be VERY sensitive — child removal is one of the most traumatic experiences a parent can have. Validate the experience without making assumptions. Family members can speak for the parent. Route to DOJ + AZ Center for Disability Law + The Arc of AZ. For cases still ongoing, urge immediate legal help (Maricopa County Bar Lawyer Referral Service, AZ Office of Public Defense Services for indigent representation).

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: DCS case file number and assigned-worker identification, dependency-court case number, written accommodation requests and responses, communication records with DCS staff, court orders and findings, service plans and reunification records, medical and behavioral-health documentation establishing qualifying disability, adaptive-services or assistive-technology records.
documentation_required_simple:
- Names and dates of any DCS workers involved.
- DCS case number if you have it.
- Court documents from any hearings.
- Letters, emails, or texts from DCS or DCS contractors.
- Records of accommodations you asked for and what happened.
- Medical or therapy records about your disability.
- Records about your children and where they are.
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on Arizona parents with disabilities subjected to DCS proceedings.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You're a parent (current or former) with any disability — physical, intellectual, mental health, sensory, cognitive, or other.
- You've had any DCS involvement: an open investigation, a removal, a hearing, a reunification process, or termination of parental rights.
- You believe DCS treated you differently because of your disability OR failed to provide an accommodation you needed.

This is investigation, not litigation — there's no class to join. But your story strengthens DOJ's case.

Caregivers, grandparents, foster parents, kinship caregivers, and other family members of parents with disabilities can also report on the parent's behalf.
eligibility_professional:
Parents with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who have been subjected to AZ DCS investigations, dependency proceedings, removal proceedings, reunification services, or termination of parental rights may submit experiences to support the federal investigation.

Standing for individualized civil-rights claims may also exist under § 1983 (Equal Protection, Due Process), 42 U.S.C. § 12132, and 29 U.S.C. § 794. Such claims are subject to statutes of limitations (generally 2 years in Arizona for federal civil-rights claims).
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You're a parent in Arizona.
- You have a disability of any kind.
- DCS has been involved with your family — they investigated, they took your child, you had hearings, you had reunification services, etc.
- You think DCS treated you unfairly because of your disability, or didn't give you the help you needed.

Family members can also share the story on behalf of a parent.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Documentation strategy: contemporaneous written records of all DCS interactions; preservation of all written communications; explicit written accommodation requests creating administrative record; service-plan and case-plan preservation; medical-records preservation; coordination with disability-rights counsel for systemic pattern documentation.

For § 1983 claims, statute of limitations runs from each discrete discriminatory act; preserve documentation of each independently.
evidence_guidance_simple:
Going forward:
- Write down each DCS interaction — date, who you spoke to, what was said, what was decided.
- Save every letter, email, text from DCS or your DCS-assigned services (counselors, parenting classes, supervised visits).
- If you ask for an accommodation (interpreter, easier-to-read documents, transportation help, etc.) — write it down even if you ask in person. Get the worker's name and what they said.
- Keep records of any service plan or reunification plan they give you.
- If you have a lawyer, give them everything.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the Arizona Department of Child Safety (DCS), opened in December 2024.

DOJ is investigating allegations that DCS systematically discriminates against parents with disabilities — physical, intellectual, mental health, and other — in child welfare proceedings. The investigation focuses on:
- DCS investigation practices that treat disability as evidence of unfitness without proper assessment.
- Failures to provide reasonable accommodations during DCS investigations, hearings, and family-reunification services.
- Communication failures with parents who are deaf, blind, or have cognitive disabilities.
- Use of disability status as grounds for removal or termination of parental rights when the actual safety concern doesn't require removal.
- Inadequate adaptive parenting services and supports.

This pattern — disability discrimination in child welfare — has been the subject of major DOJ enforcement against multiple states (most notably Massachusetts in 2015, and others). Arizona is the current focus.

The investigation is in active phase. Parents with disabilities (and their advocates) who have had children removed or have experienced DCS proceedings without accommodation should report their experiences to support the investigation.

Note: this is not a class action — there's no formal class to join. But DOJ uses individual stories to build the case for a settlement or lawsuit against AZ DCS.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division opened an investigation of the Arizona Department of Child Safety in December 2024 under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing alleged systemic disability discrimination in child welfare proceedings.

The investigation framework — building on the 2015 Massachusetts DCF settlement and analogous matters — examines: (i) the use of disability status as proxy evidence of parental unfitness without individualized functional assessment; (ii) failures to provide reasonable accommodations (qualified interpreters, accessible documents, communication accommodations) during DCS investigations, dependency proceedings, and reunification services; (iii) inadequate adaptive parenting services and assistive technology supports; (iv) communication barriers with parents who are deaf, blind, or have cognitive disabilities; (v) over-reliance on removal where less-restrictive supports would address safety concerns.

The investigation remains in active phase — no consent decree or litigation yet. Parents with disabilities subjected to DCS proceedings should report contemporaneously to support the federal evidence record.

Reports may proceed via: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov); Arizona Center for Disability Law (state Protection & Advocacy entity); The Arc of Arizona; private family/dependency-law counsel; § 1983 actions for specific civil-rights violations may proceed in parallel.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is looking into Arizona's child welfare agency. They opened the investigation in December 2024.

DOJ thinks that Arizona DCS is treating parents unfairly because of their disabilities. They are taking children away from parents who have disabilities — like physical disabilities, mental health conditions, intellectual disabilities, or being deaf or blind — without giving parents the help they need to parent safely.

Examples of what DOJ is looking at:
- Sign language interpreters not provided to deaf parents at meetings or hearings.
- Reading materials given to parents with intellectual disabilities in formats they can't read.
- DCS deciding a parent isn't fit just because of a mental health condition, without looking at what they actually do.
- Children removed when actually the parent just needed support, not separation.

If this has happened to you or your family in Arizona, you can tell DOJ. They use real stories to build their case.

Where to tell your story:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free, online).
- The Arizona Center for Disability Law (the state P&A).
- The Arc of Arizona.
- A family lawyer if you have one.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement litigation
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

no_documentation_path_professional:
Reports may proceed without contemporaneous records. Parents may request their own DCS case files under A.R.S. § 8-807 and federal disclosure rules. Counsel-mediated access to records is also available.

Reporting channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division; AZ Center for Disability Law (state P&A); The Arc of Arizona; private family-law and § 1983 counsel; HHS Administration for Children and Families (federal oversight of state child welfare).
no_documentation_path_simple:
You don't need a lot of paperwork. Many parents don't have access to DCS records, especially after children are removed.

Where to go:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Arizona Center for Disability Law: 1-800-927-2260.
- The Arc of Arizona.
- A family-law attorney can request your DCS records for you under disclosure rules.

Even just writing down what happened in your own words — date, what DCS did, why you think it's discrimination — is enough to start.
short_description: DOJ investigating AZ DCS over alleged discrimination against parents with disabilities in child welfare proceedings.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II and Section 504 of Arizona Department of Child Safety. December 2024 investigation opened over alleged discrimination against parents with disabilities in child welfare proceedings, including failures to provide reasonable accommodations during investigations, hearings, and reunification services.
short_description_simple: The U.S. Department of Justice is investigating Arizona's Department of Child Safety. DOJ thinks DCS is taking children from parents with disabilities without proper accommodations. If this has happened to you in Arizona, share your story.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: out-of-state DCS proceedings; ICWA-governed tribal proceedings; TPR appeals (separate appellate framework); foster-parent civil-rights claims (separate § 1983 framework); adoption disputes (state probate); other AZ agency discrimination (separate Title II claims).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- DCS proceedings outside Arizona (other states have separate frameworks; some have ongoing DOJ enforcement).
- Tribal child welfare (ICWA frameworks are separate).
- Termination-of-parental-rights appeals (separate court process; need family-law counsel).
- Foster parent grievances against DCS.
- Adoption-related disputes.
- Discrimination by other AZ state agencies (separate Title II claims).

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Confirm FlixBus or Greyhound. Route to DOJ ADA.gov + FMCSA (1-800-FORCE-1) + DRA. Emphasize the 48-hour advance reservation requirement — if user wasn't told about it, that's itself important evidence.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: trip records (origin, destination, date, ticket/reservation confirmation); driver identification and bus identification (vehicle number); description of accommodation requested and response; photographic evidence of inoperable lift or inaccessible terminal facilities; communications with carrier customer service; FMCSA complaint records if filed; alternative-transportation expenses incurred as result of carrier failure.
documentation_required_simple:
- The trip route (city to city).
- The date.
- Your ticket or reservation number.
- What kind of barrier you had.
- Driver name (if you have it).
- Photos of the bus or terminal (if you have them).
- Any emails or texts with the carrier.
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on intercity bus riders with mobility, sensory, or other disabilities who use FlixBus or Greyhound.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You have a disability that affects travel — wheelchair use, mobility impairment, sensory disability, etc.
- You tried to take a FlixBus or Greyhound trip (or wanted to) in the past 2-3 years.
- You experienced any of these barriers:
  - The wheelchair lift on the bus didn't work or the driver refused to deploy it.
  - You couldn't reserve accessible service through the booking system.
  - You were stranded at a terminal because the promised accessible bus didn't show up or wasn't accessible.
  - You were charged extra or had additional documentation demanded for accessibility.
  - You experienced rudeness or discrimination from drivers or staff.

This is investigation, not litigation. Your story strengthens DOJ's case.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who used or attempted to use FlixBus or Greyhound intercity bus service may submit experiences to support the federal investigation. Both DOJ Title III and FMCSA DOT-regulation enforcement avenues are available; § 1983 actions are not available against private carriers, but Title III private rights of action provide for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees.
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You have a disability that affects how you travel (wheelchair, walker, vision loss, etc.).
- You took (or tried to take) a FlixBus or Greyhound bus.
- Something went wrong with the lift, the driver, the reservation, or the bus itself.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Pre-travel: secure documented 48-hour advance reservation for accessible service per 49 C.F.R. § 37.209; preserve all confirmation records.

Field: bus identification (vehicle number, if visible), driver identification, contemporaneous photographic capture of lift mechanism and terminal facilities, written accommodation requests (where possible).

Post-travel: file parallel complaints (DOJ + FMCSA + state AG); document alternative-transportation expenses for compensatory-damages support; coordinate with disability-rights counsel for systemic pattern documentation.
evidence_guidance_simple:
Before traveling:
- Make your accessible-service reservation at least 48 hours ahead. The rules require this advance notice. Get a confirmation.
- Save the confirmation email or screenshot.

At the terminal:
- Photograph the bus (especially the lift) and any inaccessible terminal features if you have time.
- Get the driver's name and the bus number.

If a barrier happens:
- Write down everything they said — exact words help.
- Take a photo if you safely can.
- Note your alternative arrangements (had to call a friend, took a different bus, etc.) and the cost.

After:
- Report immediately to DOJ + FMCSA while it's fresh.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. — two of the largest intercity bus carriers in the United States. FlixBus acquired Greyhound in 2021, consolidating the brands under a single corporate parent.

Intercity buses are covered by the ADA AND by Department of Transportation regulations at 49 C.F.R. Part 37 (Over-the-Road Bus Accessibility), which is a more demanding framework than Title III alone. The regulations require:
- Wheelchair lifts on every bus.
- 48-hour-advance reservation for accessible service (so the carrier can deploy an accessible bus).
- Accessible reservation systems (phone and online).
- Trained drivers who can operate the lift and secure mobility devices.

DOJ is investigating allegations that FlixBus and Greyhound systemically fail to comply: wheelchair lifts that aren't maintained or operable; drivers who refuse to deploy the lift, claiming it's broken or they're not trained; reservation systems that don't let wheelchair users actually reserve accessible service; passengers left stranded at terminals when the lift doesn't work or the bus that arrives isn't the promised accessible bus.

The investigation is in active phase — not yet resolved by consent decree or litigation. Riders who've experienced these barriers should report.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division opened an investigation of FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and the Department of Transportation accessibility regulations at 49 C.F.R. Part 37 (specifically pt. 37 subpt. H for over-the-road buses). FlixBus acquired Greyhound in 2021, creating a consolidated intercity bus carrier subject to integrated enforcement.

DOT regulations impose stringent obligations on over-the-road bus carriers: 49 C.F.R. § 37.7 (entity-wide accessibility requirements); § 37.165 (lift-and-securement equipment on all vehicles); § 37.171 (driver training); § 37.209 (specific advance-reservation procedures for accessible service); § 37.211 (accessible-vehicle deployment for advance-reserved trips); § 37.215 (terminal accessibility).

DOJ is investigating allegations of systemic non-compliance: lift unavailability/inoperability; driver refusal of accommodation citing "lift broken" or "not trained"; reservation-system failures preventing wheelchair users from completing 48-hour advance accessible-service reservations; passenger stranding at terminals when scheduled accessible service is not provided; and inadequate complaint-handling and remediation.

The investigation is in active phase. Riders may report to DOJ, FMCSA (DOT enforcement, 1-800-FORCE-1), private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN), and state civil-rights agencies. Title III provides for injunctive relief and fees; DOT regulations may support administrative remediation.
full_description_simple:
DOJ is looking into two big intercity bus companies: FlixBus and Greyhound. (FlixBus bought Greyhound a few years ago, so they're really one company now.)

People who use wheelchairs say:
- The lifts on the buses don't work.
- Drivers say "the lift is broken" or "I can't use it" and leave wheelchair users at the bus station.
- It's hard to reserve an accessible seat 48 hours ahead like the rules say.
- The website and phone line don't handle accessible bookings well.

Intercity buses have stricter rules than other businesses. They have to have lifts. They have to have drivers who can use the lifts. They have to let you reserve accessible service.

If this has happened to you, you can:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Call DOJ Disability Rights Section: 800-514-0301.
- File with the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (DOT's bus regulator).
- Report through your state attorney general.

key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement or DOJ enforcement; FMCSA administrative action also possible
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without contemporaneous records. DOJ has independent subpoena authority; FMCSA may compel carrier records production. Reporting channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov); FMCSA enforcement (1-800-FORCE-1); state AG civil-rights divisions; private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN).
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without records:

- FMCSA (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration): 1-800-FORCE-1.
- Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org.
- Your state attorney general.

Even just describing what happened — "Greyhound Phoenix to Tucson, January 2025, driver refused to use the lift" — is enough to start.
short_description: DOJ investigating intercity bus carriers over wheelchair accommodation failures and inaccessible booking. Share your experience.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title III and 49 C.F.R. Part 37 of FlixBus, Inc. and Greyhound Lines, Inc. (intercity over-the-road bus carriers). Investigation addresses wheelchair-lift availability and operability, drivers' refusal of accommodation, accessible-booking system failures, and over-the-road bus accessibility deficiencies under DOT regulations.

what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: other intercity bus carriers (separate Title III/DOT enforcement); local fixed-route transit (Title II framework); paratransit (49 C.F.R. § 37.121); charter/tour bus operators (different regulatory framework); Amtrak (separate enforcement context); airlines (ACAA framework, not ADA).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Other bus operators (Megabus, Trailways, Peter Pan, etc. — separate enforcement).
- Local transit (city buses, paratransit) — different framework.
- Charter buses or tour buses — different regulations.
- Train (Amtrak) — Amtrak has its own ADA obligations and disputes.
- Airlines — different framework (Air Carrier Access Act, not ADA).

questions.prompt: Are you (or your family member) an Idaho resident?
Is the person currently in a nursing home, ICF, state hospital, or other institution in Idaho?
Does the person want to live at home or in the community? Have they expressed this preference?
questions.purpose: Establishes the Olmstead claim — institutionalization must be unwanted.

questions.prompt: Is the person on a waiting list for Medicaid home- and community-based services (HCBS)? For how long?
How did the person end up in the institution? (Hospital discharge? Family couldn't manage care? Crisis?)
questions.purpose: Identifies entry pathway — discharge defaults are a key DOJ concern.

questions.prompt: Have you or the person asked Idaho Medicaid or anyone else to help with a transition home? What happened?
questions.purpose: Establishes specific accommodation requests and responses.

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Be VERY sensitive — institutionalization affects deeply personal autonomy and family dynamics. Validate both the person's desire to live in the community AND the family's real concerns about care needs. Route to DOJ + DisAbility Rights Idaho + long-term care ombudsman. For ongoing situations, urge connection to a CIL or transition coordinator immediately — there are sometimes more services available than people realize.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: facility identification and admission date; Medicaid eligibility records (Medicaid number, HCBS waiver application and waitlist status); hospital discharge records and discharge-planning notes; service-plan documentation; assessment records (level-of-care determinations under 42 C.F.R. § 441.302); communications with discharge planners, social workers, ombudsman, Adult Protective Services; medical records establishing the qualifying disability and care needs.
documentation_required_simple:
- The facility name and location.
- Date the person entered the facility (or was first put on a waitlist).
- What services they need or asked for at home.
- Records of any Medicaid waiver applications, denials, or waitlists.
- Hospital discharge papers if they came from a hospital.
- Names of social workers, discharge planners, or Medicaid coordinators.
eligibility:
The investigation focuses on Idaho residents with disabilities who are institutionalized or at serious risk of institutionalization.

You can support the investigation by sharing your experience if:
- You (or a family member) are currently in an Idaho nursing facility, ICF/IID (intermediate care facility for intellectual disabilities), state psychiatric hospital, or similar institutional setting.
- You (or the family member) want to leave the institution but can't get sufficient community-based services to make that possible.
- You're on a Medicaid HCBS (home- and community-based services) waiver waitlist.
- You were discharged from a hospital to a nursing facility when community placement would have been appropriate.
- You were discouraged or actively diverted from community placement by Idaho's Medicaid system, your discharge planner, or state agency staff.

Family members, advocates, and guardians can submit on behalf of the affected person.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who are currently institutionalized in Idaho nursing facilities, ICF/IIDs, or state psychiatric facilities, or are at serious risk of institutionalization due to inadequate community-based service capacity, may submit experiences supporting the federal Olmstead investigation. Family, guardians, and advocates may submit on the affected person's behalf.
eligibility_simple:
You can share your story if:
- You (or your family member) is in an Idaho nursing home, ICF, state hospital, or institution.
- You (or they) want to leave but can't get enough services at home.
- You're on a waiting list for home-based services.
- You went from the hospital to a nursing home and feel like they didn't even try to send you home with help.

Family members can speak for the person.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Documentation strategy: contemporaneous records of Medicaid HCBS waiver applications, denials, and waitlist positions; preservation of discharge-planning records; service-plan and care-plan preservation; written transition requests by the individual or guardian to facility staff and state agencies; preservation of medical and functional-assessment records.

For individual Olmstead-relief actions: private rights of action under 28 C.F.R. § 35.130(d) (integration mandate) are available for affected individuals; statute of limitations runs from each discrete refusal of community placement.
evidence_guidance_simple:
Going forward:
- Write down each Medicaid or waiver communication — date, who you talked to, what they said.
- Save denial letters, waitlist notifications, and service-plan documents.
- Keep a record of how long the person has been on a waitlist.
- If the person wants to leave the facility, ask their care team to document the request and the response.
- File a complaint with DOJ while it's fresh.
full_description:
This is an active U.S. Department of Justice investigation into the State of Idaho, opened with a January 2025 findings letter. The case is about Idaho's system for serving people with disabilities — specifically, people who could live in their own homes or in community-based settings but are instead placed in or stuck in nursing facilities.

The case is grounded in Olmstead v. L.C., a landmark 1999 U.S. Supreme Court decision interpreting the ADA. Olmstead held that unjustified institutionalization of people with disabilities — when community-based services would be appropriate — is illegal disability discrimination.

DOJ alleges that Idaho's system fails Olmstead in multiple ways:
- Inadequate Medicaid home- and community-based services (HCBS) — long waitlists, restrictive eligibility, insufficient capacity to actually serve people in their homes.
- Hospital and nursing-facility discharge planning that defaults to long-term institutional placement even when the person could go home with appropriate supports.
- Insufficient supports for people with intellectual and developmental disabilities, serious mental illness, and complex medical needs.
- A bias in funding and policy that makes institutionalization the path of least resistance.

The investigation is in active phase. Olmstead-grounded DOJ investigations have led to major statewide reform settlements in many states (Connecticut, Georgia, Mississippi, North Carolina, Virginia, others). Idaho is the current focus.

If you (or a family member) is currently institutionalized in Idaho — in a nursing home, ICF/IID, state hospital, or similar — or has been on a waitlist for community services, your story supports the investigation.
full_description_professional:
The DOJ Civil Rights Division issued a findings letter in January 2025 alleging that the State of Idaho violates Title II of the ADA and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, as interpreted in Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999), through unnecessary segregation of persons with disabilities in nursing facilities, intermediate care facilities for individuals with intellectual disabilities (ICF/IID), and state psychiatric facilities, when community-based services would be both appropriate and preferred.

The investigation framework follows the established DOJ Olmstead enforcement template — building on settlements with Connecticut, Georgia (DOJ v. State of Georgia, 2010), Mississippi (United States v. State of Mississippi, 2021), North Carolina, and Virginia — examining: (i) Medicaid HCBS waiver capacity and waitlists under 42 C.F.R. Part 441 Subpart G; (ii) hospital and SNF discharge-planning practices and defaults; (iii) crisis-services and pre-institutional-placement diversion capacity; (iv) state administrative and budgetary biases favoring institutional over community-based services; (v) inadequate transition supports for currently-institutionalized persons seeking community placement.

The investigation is in active phase. Resolution path involves either voluntary settlement (typical pattern: 5-10 year reform commitments with HCBS expansion targets, waitlist reduction, and quality monitoring) or DOJ enforcement litigation under 42 U.S.C. § 12133.

Stakeholders include: DisAbility Rights Idaho (state P&A); the National Disability Rights Network; CMS (federal Medicaid oversight); Idaho Department of Health and Welfare; local Centers for Independent Living. Reports to DOJ may proceed via ADA.gov; coordinate with DRI for systemic pattern documentation and individual transition advocacy.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice is looking into the State of Idaho. They opened the investigation in January 2025.

DOJ says Idaho is sending people with disabilities to nursing homes when they could live at home or in their own community with help.

This is illegal. A Supreme Court case called Olmstead (from 1999) said that states can't put people with disabilities in nursing homes or institutions if those people could live in the community with the right help. It's a form of disability discrimination.

DOJ thinks Idaho:
- Doesn't have enough home-care services to actually let people live at home.
- Has waitlists that are too long for community services.
- Sends people from the hospital straight to nursing homes instead of helping them go home.
- Puts people with intellectual disabilities, serious mental illness, or complicated medical needs in institutions when they could be in the community.

If you (or your family member) is in an Idaho nursing home, hospital, or institution and wants to leave — or has been on a waitlist for home services — you can tell DOJ. Your story helps.

Where to share:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- DisAbility Rights Idaho.
- A Medicaid waiver coordinator.

key_dates.Precedent: Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999); DOJ settlements with GA (2010), MS (2021), NC, VA, CT
key_dates.Resolution path: Voluntary settlement (typical: 5-10 year reform commitment) or DOJ enforcement litigation
key_dates.Status: Active investigation; no consent decree or litigation yet

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without contemporaneous records. DOJ has subpoena authority for Idaho state records. Reporting channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov); DisAbility Rights Idaho (state P&A); Idaho long-term care ombudsman; Centers for Independent Living; CMS for Medicaid-specific concerns; private Olmstead counsel where individual injunctive relief is sought.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can share your story without all the paperwork:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free, online).
- DisAbility Rights Idaho: 1-866-262-3462.
- The Idaho long-term care ombudsman.
- The Centers for Independent Living in Idaho.

Family members can act on behalf of an institutionalized person. Even a brief description — "my mother went into the nursing home in 2024, she wants to come home, but they can't find a way to make it work" — is enough to start.
short_description: DOJ findings letter alleges Idaho needlessly segregates people with disabilities in nursing facilities instead of community-based services. Share your experience to support the investigation.
short_description_professional: Active DOJ Civil Rights Division investigation under ADA Title II and Section 504 of State of Idaho, addressing unnecessary segregation of persons with disabilities in nursing facilities in violation of Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999). January 2025 findings letter alleges Idaho's service system funnels persons with disabilities into institutional placements when community-based alternatives would be appropriate and preferred.
short_description_simple: DOJ is investigating Idaho. DOJ says Idaho is keeping people with disabilities in nursing homes when they could live in their own homes or communities with help. This violates a Supreme Court case called Olmstead. If you or a family member is stuck in an Idaho nursing facility, share your story.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: out-of-state institutional placements; care-quality complaints under separate state-survey and federal-CMS frameworks; Medicaid eligibility disputes (separate administrative-hearing framework); private long-term-care insurance; guardianship/conservatorship disputes (state probate); SNF Medicare-coverage disputes (CMS appeals).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Nursing homes outside Idaho.
- Care-quality complaints unrelated to Olmstead (separate ombudsman process, state survey complaints).
- General Medicaid eligibility disputes.
- Private long-term-care insurance disputes.
- Family conflict over guardianship or placement decisions (separate probate/family-law framework).

questions.prompt: Were you a tenant in Anoka, Minnesota?
If not Anoka — does your city have a crime-free housing program? (Many do; check with your local landlord or city council.)
Were police called to your home (by you, family, or others)? How many times in the past year?
Were any of those calls related to a mental health crisis or condition?
Did your landlord threaten eviction, give you notice, or actually evict you because of those police contacts?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Be sensitive — mental health crises and housing loss are deeply intertwined trauma. Validate the experience. For Anoka residents, route to MN Legal Aid + DOJ. For other cities, explain the precedent and route to DOJ + state P&A. If user is currently facing eviction, escalate urgency: emergency legal help is the priority.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: lease agreements, landlord notices invoking crime-free housing terms, police-contact records (911 logs, incident reports), municipal ordinance text, eviction-court records, medical documentation establishing the qualifying mental health disability, correspondence between landlord and municipal licensing authority.
documentation_required_simple:
- Your lease and any landlord notices about police-contact concerns.
- Police reports or 911 records (most are public — you can request them).
- Medical or mental health records establishing the qualifying disability.
- Any communication from your landlord about the crime-free program.
- Eviction notices, if any.
eligibility:
The settlement directly binds the City of Anoka, MN. It does not create class membership for residents of other municipalities.

You can report a problem under this settlement if:
- You were a tenant in Anoka.
- You were threatened with eviction, evicted, or had your landlord pressured by Anoka's crime-free housing program.
- The police-contact incident(s) were related to a mental health disability — yours, a family member's, or another household resident's.

For residents of other cities with crime-free housing programs:
- This case is precedent. You can use it to support a DOJ complaint about your own city.
- DOJ accepts Title II complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170 about any municipality.
- Mental health and Section 504 protections apply to federally funded municipalities specifically.
eligibility_professional: Direct settlement coverage extends to tenants in Anoka, MN affected by the crime-free housing ordinance's pre-2024 enforcement. Persons in analogous jurisdictions may invoke the settlement as supportive precedent in DOJ administrative complaints and § 1983 actions challenging similar programs.
eligibility_simple:
You can report under THIS specific settlement if:
- You lived in Anoka, Minnesota as a tenant.
- Your landlord tried to evict you (or did evict you) because police were called.
- The police calls were because of a mental health crisis.

If you live in a DIFFERENT city with a similar "crime-free housing" program, this case is still useful — you can use it to support your own DOJ complaint. Many cities have these programs.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Documentation strategy: contemporaneous preservation of all landlord and municipal communications; FOIA/public-records requests for police-contact records and ordinance-enforcement records; medical-records preservation establishing qualifying disability and disability-driven causation for police contacts; preservation of eviction-court records and any judgment or settlement.

For prospective claims: any new crime-free-housing-related notice or contact creates a discrete actionable event. Document each independently with dates, parties, and content.
evidence_guidance_simple:
Track everything:
- Save lease, notices, eviction papers, and any letter from the city or landlord.
- Request your 911/police records — they're usually public.
- Get medical records showing your (or family member's) mental health condition.
- Note dates of every police interaction and what it was about.

For documenting going forward:
- If your landlord or city sends ANY notice tied to police contacts, keep it. Photograph or scan it.
- If you're facing eviction, file for emergency housing legal help — many state legal-aid orgs handle these.
full_description:
This is a landmark 2024 DOJ enforcement settlement with the City of Anoka, Minnesota. The case addresses a category of municipal program — "crime-free housing" — that exists in hundreds of U.S. cities. These programs require landlords to evict tenants over interactions with police, including calls for help during a mental health crisis.

DOJ found that Anoka's crime-free housing program, as implemented, illegally targeted tenants with mental health disabilities. People calling 911 during psychiatric crises, family members calling for welfare checks, or any interaction with police related to disability-driven behavior triggered eviction proceedings. The result: tenants with serious mental illness lost their housing precisely when they most needed it.

The settlement requires Anoka to:
- End the practice of counting mental-health-related police contacts as "criminal activity" for purposes of the ordinance.
- Provide reasonable accommodations to tenants who interact with police due to a disability.
- Train landlords and police on disability-rights obligations.
- Audit prior enforcement and remediate evictions that violated the settlement standards.
- Submit to DOJ monitoring.

The case sets enforcement precedent for similar crime-free housing programs nationwide. If your city has a similar program and you've been pushed out (or threatened) because of disability-driven police contacts, the DOJ takes complaints — and this case provides direct supportive precedent.
full_description_professional:
United States v. City of Anoka, Minnesota (D. Minn., 2024 settlement), is a landmark DOJ enforcement action under Title II ADA addressing the disability-discriminatory enforcement of municipal "crime-free housing" ordinances. The case targets a category of municipal program existing in hundreds of jurisdictions that conditions landlord licenses on tenant-eviction obligations triggered by police-contact thresholds.

DOJ found that the program's enforcement counted mental-health-disability-related police contacts (911 wellness calls, psychiatric-crisis welfare checks, behavior driven by qualifying psychiatric conditions) as "criminal activity" for ordinance purposes, resulting in eviction threats and removals targeting tenants with serious mental illness. The settlement implements §§ 28 C.F.R. 35.130 (prohibitions on disparate treatment), § 35.130(b)(7) (reasonable modifications), and § 35.130(b)(8) (eligibility criteria).

Settlement obligations: ordinance and policy revision excluding mental-health-related police contacts from "criminal activity" definitions; reasonable-accommodation pathway for tenants whose police contacts arise from qualifying disabilities; affirmative-training mandates for municipal staff and licensed landlords; retrospective audit and remediation of prior enforcement; DOJ monitoring.

The settlement establishes enforcement template for analogous claims against other municipalities. Affected tenants in other crime-free-housing jurisdictions should pursue DOJ administrative complaints and may invoke this settlement as supportive precedent.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and the City of Anoka, Minnesota from 2024.

Anoka had a program called "crime-free housing." It told landlords they had to kick out tenants if the police got called to their apartment too many times. Even if the call was someone asking for help during a mental health crisis. Even if the tenant was the one calling 911 for help.

The DOJ said this was illegal. It was punishing people for being sick.

Anoka had to:
- Stop counting calls for mental-health help as "crime."
- Help tenants stay in their homes when police are called for disability reasons.
- Train landlords and police about disability rights.
- Fix evictions that already happened because of this.

Many other cities have programs like this. If you or someone you know was pushed out of an apartment because the police were called when they were having a mental health crisis, you can report it.

key_dates.Precedent value: Template for analogous claims against other municipalities
key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

no_documentation_path_professional:
Reports may proceed without contemporaneous documentation. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170; state Protection & Advocacy agencies; state-level fair-housing enforcement agencies; legal aid organizations.

For § 1983 actions, exhaustion is not required for Title II/§ 504 claims; statute of limitations runs from the discriminatory eviction or threat.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without all the paperwork:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Contact Mid-Minnesota Legal Aid (for Anoka residents).
- Contact your local Protection & Advocacy agency.
- For other states, contact your state P&A — they're the federally funded disability-rights agency in every state.
short_description: Landmark 2025 DOJ agreement addressing crime-free housing program discrimination against tenants with mental health disabilities. Report violations elsewhere using same model.
short_description_professional: 2024 DOJ enforcement settlement against City of Anoka, MN, under ADA Title II addressing crime-free housing ordinance enforcement targeting tenants with mental health and behavioral disabilities. Compliance phase; precedent template for analogous enforcement against other municipalities operating crime-free housing programs.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with the City of Anoka, Minnesota from 2024. Anoka had a "crime-free housing" program that pushed people with mental health disabilities out of their apartments. The DOJ said this is illegal disability discrimination and Anoka had to stop. If your city has a similar program, you can use this case as a precedent to report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: jurisdictions other than Anoka, MN (though invocable as precedent); landlord-tenant disputes outside crime-free-housing context; federal housing programs (HUD/Section 8 under § 504, 24 C.F.R. pt. 8); private-landlord FHA claims; individual eviction-defense practice (requires private representation).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Cities other than Anoka, MN — but this case is precedent you can use.
- Other landlord-tenant disputes unrelated to crime-free housing programs (general evictions, lease violations).
- Federal housing (HUD, Section 8) — those have separate disability-rights enforcement under 24 C.F.R.
- Private landlord discrimination — different framework (Fair Housing Act).
- Eviction defense itself — you'd need a lawyer for that part.

questions.prompt: Do you have a disability that requires an accessible hotel room?
Did the problem happen at a Hilton-brand hotel (Hampton, DoubleTree, Embassy, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood, Conrad, Waldorf-Astoria, or others)?
What was the problem? (No accessible room when there should have been, room given to someone else, no info about accessibility, can't reserve a specific accessible room, etc.)
Do you have your reservation confirmation or screenshots?
What state was the hotel in?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Confirm Hilton-brand scope. Route to DOJ ADA.gov + state agency + Hilton customer service. Note related Niles v. Hilton case if user is dealing with bed-height/wheelchair-transfer issues.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: reservation records, confirmation numbers, brand and property identification, screenshots of online reservation interface or reservation-system descriptions, email correspondence with reservation agents, on-property documentation of room assignment and any accommodation refusals.
documentation_required_simple:
- The hotel brand and city.
- Your reservation confirmation number (if you have it).

eligibility:
The consent decree protects guests with disabilities at all Hilton-brand properties nationwide who experience accessible-room or reservation-system barriers.

You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability and require an accessible hotel room.
- You stayed at, tried to book, or had a confirmed reservation at any Hilton-brand property (Hampton, DoubleTree, Homewood, Embassy, Hilton Garden Inn, Hilton, Conrad, Waldorf-Astoria, etc.).
- The reservation system didn't describe accessibility features, didn't let you reserve a specific accessible room, gave away your accessible room to someone else, or otherwise treated accessibility differently than non-accessible reservations.

You don't need to have been part of the original DOJ enforcement to report current violations. Hilton's obligations are ongoing.
eligibility_professional:
Guests with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who encounter reservation-system or accessible-room access barriers at Hilton-brand properties retain ongoing rights under the consent decree. Coverage extends to all Hilton-owned, -operated, and -franchised brands and properties subject to Hilton's control.

Independent contractor franchisees may have limited liability exposure, but Hilton's reservation-system obligations apply system-wide regardless of property ownership.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at or tried to book any Hilton brand (Hampton, DoubleTree, Embassy, Hilton Garden Inn, etc.).
- The hotel did not let you book a specific accessible room, did not say what makes the room accessible, or gave away your accessible room.

You can report this even though the deal is from 2010 — it is still in effect.
evidence_guidance_professional: Prospective documentation: capture reservation-system screenshots showing accessibility feature descriptions (or absence), confirmation records, room-type specificity. Document any on-property variances from confirmed reservation. Preserve all email and chat correspondence with reservation agents and property staff. File administrative complaints contemporaneously while records remain accessible.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When booking:
- Screenshot the reservation pages showing accessibility (or lack of) information.
- Save confirmation emails.
- Note what specific accessible room (if any) was offered.

At check-in:
- Document any room change, refusal, or upgrade attempts.
- Photograph the actual room you're given vs. what was promised.

After:
- File a DOJ complaint while details are fresh.
full_description:
This is a federal consent decree between the U.S. Department of Justice and Hilton Worldwide, entered in 2010 in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia (1:10-cv-01902).

The DOJ sued Hilton because guests with mobility disabilities couldn't reserve accessible rooms in the same way other guests could. Many Hilton hotels and brands didn't have proper accessible rooms; when accessible rooms existed, the online and phone reservation systems often couldn't guarantee a specific accessible room, didn't describe what made a room accessible, or offered fewer choices than were available to non-disabled guests.

The consent decree requires Hilton and all of its brands (Hampton Inn, DoubleTree, Homewood Suites, Embassy Suites, Hilton Garden Inn, etc.) to:
- Build accessible rooms to the ADA Standards.
- Ensure the reservation system describes accessibility features of each accessible room.
- Allow guests to reserve a SPECIFIC accessible room (like blocking a specific non-accessible room).
- Hold accessible rooms for guests who request them.
- Provide accessibility info that's as detailed as info available about non-accessible rooms.

The consent decree is in long-term compliance phase. If you've had problems booking an accessible room at any Hilton-brand property, you can report it.

This is the consent decree referenced by Niles v. Hilton — Niles is a current case alleging continued bed-height/wheelchair-transfer issues that the 2010 decree didn't directly address.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc., 1:10-cv-01902 (D.D.C., consent decree entered Nov. 9, 2010), is a settled enforcement action under Title III of the ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., addressing Hilton's accessible-room design and reservation-system practices across all Hilton-brand properties (Hampton, DoubleTree, Homewood, Embassy, Garden Inn, Hilton, Conrad, Waldorf-Astoria, etc.).

The consent decree binds Hilton to: (i) construct accessible rooms to ADA Standards; (ii) describe accessibility features of accessible rooms in reservation systems; (iii) permit guests to reserve specific accessible rooms with the same specificity as non-accessible rooms; (iv) hold accessible rooms for guests requesting them; (v) ensure reservation parity in detail and specificity; (vi) train reservation staff; (vii) submit to ongoing monitoring and reporting.

The decree has informed subsequent DOJ regulatory action under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e) (reservation systems requirements) and serves as the template for subsequent hospitality-industry enforcement (Marriott 2024 settlement, etc.).

Compliance phase ongoing. Reports of non-compliance — inaccessible-to-book accessible rooms, missing accessibility descriptions, refusals to honor specific-room reservations — may be made to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), via private Title III actions, or state civil-rights agencies.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Hilton from 2010. It is still in effect.

The DOJ sued Hilton because people with disabilities could not book accessible rooms the same way other people booked regular rooms. Some Hiltons did not have accessible rooms. Some did, but the website would not say which rooms were accessible, or would not let you reserve a specific accessible room.

Hilton agreed to:
- Build accessible rooms.
- Put information about accessible rooms on their website.
- Let you reserve a specific accessible room (not just "an accessible room").
- Hold the room for you so they don't give it to someone else.
- Make accessible-room info as detailed as regular-room info.

This applies to all Hilton brands — Hampton Inn, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hilton Garden Inn, and many others.

If you have trouble booking an accessible room at any Hilton, you can report it. The U.S. Department of Justice will take complaints.

key_dates.Related case: Niles v. Hilton (2026, ongoing — bed-height issues not addressed in 2010 decree)
key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing

no_documentation_path_professional:
Reports may proceed without contemporaneous documentation. DOJ retains independent enforcement authority and can subpoena Hilton records.

Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), state civil-rights agencies, individual Title III actions, parallel state-law actions (CA Unruh, NY Human Rights Law, etc.).
no_documentation_path_simple:
Even without documentation, you can report:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Contact Hilton's customer service directly — and save their response.
- File with your state attorney general.
- For California stays, file with CA Civil Rights Department (Unruh Act).
short_description: 2010 DOJ consent decree requires Hilton to make rooms accessible and reservation systems compatible. Compliance phase — report current violations that should be covered.
short_description_professional: 2010 DOJ consent decree (1:10-cv-01902, D.D.C.) binding Hilton Worldwide and Hilton-brand properties to accessible-room design and reservation-system parity under ADA Title III. Compliance phase; ongoing enforcement available via DOJ administrative complaint and direct private actions.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Hilton from 2010. Hilton has to make their rooms accessible and make sure people can reserve those rooms online or by phone — just like any other room. If you're having trouble with this at any Hilton, you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: non-Hilton hospitality brands (Marriott has separate 2024 settlement; Hyatt, IHG, Wyndham, Choice, Best Western under separate enforcement frameworks), vacation-rental platforms, cruise lines, on-property restaurants/spas/fitness facilities (separate Title III obligations), past-events damages.
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Non-Hilton hotels (Marriott has its own separate settlement, Hyatt, IHG, Wyndham, etc. have their own obligations).
- Vacation rentals (Airbnb, VRBO).
- Cruise lines, even Hilton-affiliated.
- Hotel restaurants (separate Title III issues for restaurants).
- Pool/gym/spa accessibility (separate Title III claims for fitness facilities).
- Past money damages. The consent decree changes future behavior.

questions.prompt: Are you a registered voter in LA County?
Do you have a disability that affects how you vote?
What barrier did you encounter at the vote center? (Building access, voting machine, curbside denied, poll-worker conduct, etc.)
Did you ask for curbside voting? What did they say?
questions.purpose: Core settlement obligation; curbside availability is required.

questions.prompt: When did this happen? Which election?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Confirm LA County scope. Route to DOJ + LA County Registrar + Disability Rights California. Curbside voting denial is a per-se settlement violation — flag it explicitly.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: vote-center location, date and time of attempt, specific barrier(s) encountered (physical access, equipment functionality, curbside availability, poll-worker conduct), poll-worker identification where available, any contemporaneous photographs, and records of complaints filed with the Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk.
documentation_required_simple:
- The vote center address and date.
- Description of the barrier.
- Whether you were offered curbside voting and what happened if so.
- Your name and disability (if you're comfortable sharing).
- Any complaint you already filed with the Registrar.
eligibility:
The settlement protects voters with disabilities encountering accessibility barriers at LA County vote centers.

You can report a problem if:
- You're a registered voter in LA County.
- You have any disability — mobility, vision, hearing, cognitive, etc.
- You encountered a barrier when voting at an LA County vote center: couldn't enter the building, couldn't access the parking, accessible voting machine wasn't working or wasn't set up, curbside voting wasn't offered or was inadequate, poll workers were unhelpful or untrained.

This is LA County-specific. Other California counties have their own obligations; statewide claims would proceed separately.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities registered to vote in Los Angeles County who encounter Title II/HAVA-implicating barriers at vote centers retain reporting and enforcement rights under the settlement and concurrent federal/state law.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You vote in LA County.
- You have a disability.
- You had trouble voting at a vote center — couldn't get in, the machine wasn't working, no one offered you curbside voting, etc.

This is for LA County only. Other counties in California have their own rules.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Field documentation: precise vote-center location, time, specific barrier; photographic capture (if non-disruptive to voting); poll-worker identification; explicit on-record requests for accommodations (especially curbside voting); request copies of any incident reports filed by poll workers.

Post-encounter: file parallel complaints (DOJ + LA County + state); coordinate with Disability Rights California for systemic pattern documentation.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When you encounter a barrier:
- Note the vote center address and time.
- Take a photo if you can do so safely without disrupting voting.
- Get the name of the poll workers involved.
- Ask about curbside voting explicitly. If denied, get that on record.

After:
- Report immediately while details are fresh. DOJ + LA County Registrar.
- Tell Disability Rights California.
full_description:
This is a 2024 DOJ settlement with Los Angeles County over accessibility at the County's vote centers — the polling locations LA County uses in lieu of traditional precinct polling places.

DOJ found that LA County's vote centers had pervasive accessibility barriers: inaccessible building entrances, lack of accessible parking, voting equipment that wasn't set up for blind voters or voters with mobility disabilities, and inadequate curbside-voting alternatives for voters who couldn't safely enter the building.

The settlement requires LA County to:
- Audit and remediate physical access barriers at all vote-center facilities.
- Ensure accessible voting machines are deployed and functioning at every vote center.
- Provide curbside voting at every vote center as an alternative for voters who can't enter.
- Train poll workers on accessibility obligations.
- Submit to ongoing DOJ monitoring and election-cycle reporting.

The case is in compliance phase. If you're a voter with a disability in LA County and you've encountered barriers at a vote center, you can report it — DOJ has independent enforcement authority and the LA County Registrar is bound by the settlement.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Los Angeles County (DOJ administrative settlement, August 2024), is a settled enforcement action under Title II ADA and the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081, addressing physical and programmatic accessibility deficiencies at LA County vote centers.

DOJ findings: pervasive physical-access barriers (entrances, parking, paths of travel under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and ADA Standards); accessible-voting-machine deployment and operational failures (BMD/accessible-equipment availability and functionality); inadequate or unoffered curbside-voting alternatives despite statutory mandate.

Settlement obligations: facility-level remediation per audit; consistent accessible-equipment deployment; mandated curbside-voting availability at all centers; poll-worker training; election-cycle reporting; DOJ monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Voters with disabilities encountering current barriers should report to DOJ Civil Rights Division (Voting Section, ADA.gov), LA County Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk, California Civil Rights Department, and disability-rights advocacy organizations.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and LA County from 2024.

LA County uses "vote centers" instead of regular polling places. The DOJ found that many vote centers were not accessible. Wheelchair users couldn't get in. Blind voters couldn't use the voting machines. Curbside voting (where someone brings the ballot to your car) was not really offered, even when the law said it should be.

LA County had to:
- Fix the buildings.
- Make sure accessible voting machines work at every vote center.
- Offer curbside voting at every vote center.
- Train poll workers.
- Let DOJ check up on them after each election.

If you have a disability and had trouble voting at an LA County vote center, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase; election-cycle reporting ongoing

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section; LA County Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk; California Secretary of State (Help America Vote Act administration); Disability Rights California; California Civil Rights Department.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without records:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- File with the LA County Registrar.
- Contact Disability Rights California.
- File with the California Secretary of State.
short_description: DOJ settlement August 2024 requires LA County to provide accessible vote centers and curbside voting. Report violations.
short_description_professional: August 2024 DOJ settlement with Los Angeles County under ADA Title II and HAVA addressing vote-center accessibility deficiencies and mandating curbside voting availability for voters with disabilities. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with LA County from 2024. LA County has to make sure voters with disabilities can use vote centers — including curbside voting if they can't get inside. If you've had problems voting in LA County because of accessibility, you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: other California counties (separate enforcement per county); state-level VRA claims; mail-in/RAVBM accessibility (separate Title II framework, see e.g. Bryant v. Harris County); voter registration; ID requirements; partisan-redistricting claims.
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Other California counties (each has its own obligations).
- Federal elections in other states.
- Mail-in ballot accessibility (separate issue, see Bryant v. Harris County or analogous cases for vote-by-mail accessibility).
- Voter registration issues — different framework (Voting Rights Act, NVRA).
- ID-requirement challenges.

questions.purpose: Identifies which reservation channel had the issue — parity is required across channels.

questions.prompt: Do you have your reservation confirmation or screenshots?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Confirm Marriott-brand scope. Route to DOJ ADA.gov + state agency. Channel parity is a specific settlement obligation — if user encountered different info on website vs. phone, that's a flag-worthy violation.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: reservation records (confirmation number, booking channel), brand and property identification, reservation-system screenshots showing accessibility descriptions (or absence), email correspondence with reservation staff, on-property documentation of room assignment and any variances from confirmed reservation.
documentation_required_simple:
- The Marriott brand and property city.
- Your reservation confirmation.
- Screenshots showing the reservation experience.
- Date.
- What was wrong.
eligibility:
The settlement protects guests with disabilities at all Marriott-brand properties nationwide who experience accessible-room or reservation-system barriers.

You can report a problem if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at, tried to book, or had a confirmed reservation at any Marriott-brand property.
- The reservation system didn't let you reserve a specific accessible room, didn't describe accessibility features, gave your room away, or treated accessibility differently than non-accessible reservations.

The settlement is brand-system-wide. It applies whether you booked through Marriott.com, the Bonvoy app, a phone agent, a travel agency, or a third-party site like Expedia or Booking.com.
eligibility_professional: Persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 encountering reservation-system or accessible-room access barriers at Marriott-portfolio properties retain ongoing rights under the settlement. Coverage applies system-wide regardless of property-level ownership (Marriott corporate-owned, franchise, or third-party operator) where Marriott reservation infrastructure is involved.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You have a disability and need an accessible room.
- You stayed at or tried to book any Marriott-brand hotel.
- The hotel did not let you book a specific accessible room, did not say what makes the room accessible, or gave away your accessible room.

Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, Fairfield Inn, SpringHill Suites, Aloft, Element, Renaissance — these are all Marriott brands and all covered.
evidence_guidance_professional: Prospective documentation: reservation-system screenshots (multiple booking channels for parity evidence), confirmation records, on-property reconciliation, communication records. File DOJ complaints contemporaneously to support pattern enforcement.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When booking:
- Screenshot the reservation pages.
- Save the confirmation email.
- Note which accessible room (if any) was offered.

At check-in:
- Document any change.
- Photograph the actual room given.

After:

full_description:
This is a 2024 DOJ settlement with Marriott International. Marriott runs many hotel brands — Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, W, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, SpringHill Suites, Fairfield Inn, AC Hotels, Aloft, Element, Renaissance, Le Méridien, St. Regis, and many more.

DOJ found that Marriott's reservation systems didn't comply with the ADA Standards for accessible-room reservations. Guests with disabilities couldn't reserve specific accessible rooms in the same way other guests could; descriptions of accessibility features were missing or inadequate; specific accessible rooms weren't being held for the guests who requested them; and the consistency between websites, mobile apps, and reservation phone lines was inconsistent.

The settlement requires Marriott to:
- Allow guests to reserve a specific accessible room with the same specificity available to non-disabled guests.
- Describe each accessible room's features (roll-in shower vs. bathtub with grab bars, type of communication features for deaf guests, etc.).
- Hold a reserved accessible room for the guest who requested it.
- Train reservation staff on accessibility obligations.
- Implement parity across all reservation channels (website, app, phone, agency, third-party platforms like Expedia/Booking.com).
- Submit to DOJ monitoring and reporting.

The settlement covers ALL Marriott brand properties. If you've had trouble booking an accessible room at any Marriott-brand hotel, you can report it.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Marriott International, Inc. (DOJ administrative settlement, 2024), is a settled enforcement action under Title III ADA addressing reservation-system practices across Marriott's portfolio of brands. The settlement parallels the 2010 Hilton consent decree template and implements ADA Title III obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e).

Binding obligations include: reservation specificity for accessible rooms; comprehensive accessibility-feature descriptions; specific-room holds; reservation channel parity (website, mobile app, phone, GDS, third-party booking platforms); staff training; ongoing DOJ monitoring and reporting.

The settlement covers Marriott's full brand portfolio (Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, W, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, SpringHill Suites, Fairfield Inn, Aloft, Element, Renaissance, Le Méridien, St. Regis, Autograph, Tribute Portfolio, Design Hotels, Edition, Bonvoy properties, etc.).

Compliance phase ongoing. Reports of non-compliance — including failure to honor specific-room reservations, inadequate accessibility descriptions, inter-channel inconsistency, or denial of reservation parity — may be made to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), via private Title III actions, or state civil-rights agencies.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Marriott from 2024.

Marriott runs many hotel brands — Marriott, Sheraton, Westin, JW Marriott, Ritz-Carlton, Courtyard, Residence Inn, Fairfield Inn, Aloft, Element, and more.

DOJ said Marriott was not letting people with disabilities book accessible rooms the same way other people book rooms. Marriott agreed to fix this.

Now Marriott has to:
- Let you reserve a specific accessible room (not just "an accessible room").
- Tell you on the website what makes the room accessible (roll-in shower, grab bars, hearing-impaired features, etc.).
- Hold the room you reserved for you.
- Train staff on accessibility.
- Make the website, mobile app, and phone line all say the same thing.

If you have trouble booking an accessible room at any Marriott brand, you can report it to DOJ at ADA.gov.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without contemporaneous documentation. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division, state civil-rights agencies, individual Title III actions, state-law parallels (Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA, etc.).
no_documentation_path_simple:
Even without documentation:
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Contact Marriott customer service directly.
- File with your state attorney general.

short_description_professional: 2024 DOJ settlement agreement binding Marriott International and its brand portfolio to accessible-room reservation-system reforms under ADA Title III. Compliance phase; ongoing enforcement via DOJ administrative complaint.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Marriott from 2024. Marriott has to fix its accessible-room booking system at all its hotels. If you can't book an accessible room at any Marriott brand, you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: non-Marriott brands (Hilton subject to separate 2010 consent decree; Hyatt, IHG, Wyndham, Choice, Best Western under separate frameworks), Marriott vacation-rental products (timeshares), cruise lines, on-property restaurants/spas/fitness facilities (separate Title III obligations), past-events damages.
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
