questions.prompt: Are you (or have you been) a patient at a Sea Mar Community Health Center in Washington state?
Do you have a disability that requires an accommodation at a healthcare visit?
What was the problem? (Couldn't access the building, no interpreter, no accessible exam chair, communication denied, etc.)
What happened? Were you turned away, made to wait, charged extra, or denied service?
When did this happen? (Is it ongoing?)
questions.purpose: Distinguishes historical claims from current violations.

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Confirm Sea Mar patient status. Route to DOJ ADA.gov + HHS OCR (FQHC-specific) + Disability Rights Washington. The Section 1557/§ 504 angle is particularly strong for FQHCs because of the federal funding tie.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: Sea Mar visit records, appointment confirmations, communications regarding accommodation requests, staff identification, contemporaneous notes of accommodation refusals, medical records establishing the qualifying disability and the accommodation need.
documentation_required_simple:
- Date of your Sea Mar visit and the location.
- What you needed (interpreter, accessible exam table, etc.).
- What happened — were you turned away, made to wait, charged extra, or denied service?
- Any notes from the staff or denial documentation.
eligibility:
The settlement protects patients with disabilities at Sea Mar Community Health Centers in Washington state.

You can report a problem if:
- You're a Sea Mar patient (current or past).
- You have a disability.
- You encountered a barrier: couldn't access the building or exam room, no ASL interpreter provided when needed, no accessible dental chair available, communication accommodations refused, etc.

This is Sea Mar-specific. Other Washington healthcare providers have separate obligations, but the same federal framework (ADA Title III + Section 504 + Section 1557 for FQHCs) applies.
eligibility_professional: Patients with qualifying disabilities (42 U.S.C. § 12102) seeking services at any Sea Mar facility in Washington state retain ongoing rights under the settlement. As an FQHC, Sea Mar is also bound by Section 504 (federal financial assistance trigger) and Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act for healthcare-specific anti-discrimination obligations.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You go (or went) to Sea Mar for dental or medical care.
- You have a disability.
- You had trouble getting accessible care — no interpreter, can't fit in the exam room with your wheelchair, no accessible dental chair, etc.

This is for Sea Mar only. But other clinics have similar rules.
evidence_guidance_professional: Documentation strategy: capture written accommodation-refusal language; preserve appointment records and any patient-portal communications; document on-site barriers with photographs where appropriate; preserve all related medical records; preserve documentation of any care actually delivered (or not) and the consequences.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When you encounter a barrier:
- Note the location and date.
- Ask for what you need in writing if possible — and save the response.
- Document any waiting time, extra charges, or denied service.
- If they say "we can't accommodate you," ask for that in writing.

After:
- Submit a complaint while it's fresh.
full_description:
This is a 2025 DOJ enforcement settlement with Sea Mar Community Health Centers, a federally qualified health center (FQHC) network operating across Washington state.

DOJ found accessibility failures at Sea Mar's dental and medical clinics:
- Wheelchair users couldn't access exam rooms or treatment areas.
- Deaf patients weren't consistently provided sign-language interpreters or other auxiliary aids.
- Accessible dental chairs and equipment weren't available or weren't maintained.
- Communication accommodations weren't consistently offered.

The settlement requires Sea Mar to:
- Audit and remediate physical access at all locations.
- Provide qualified ASL interpreters for deaf patients (no charge to the patient).
- Procure and maintain accessible dental and medical equipment.
- Train staff.
- Provide ongoing reporting to DOJ.

The case is in compliance phase. If you're a Sea Mar patient (current or past) and you've encountered accessibility barriers, you can report it.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Sea Mar Community Health Centers (DOJ administrative settlement, April 2025), is a settled enforcement action under Title III ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing accessibility deficiencies at Sea Mar's Washington-state dental and medical clinics. Sea Mar is a federally qualified health center (FQHC), implicating § 504 obligations beyond Title III alone.

DOJ findings: physical-access barriers; inconsistent provision of qualified ASL interpreters and auxiliary aids; absence of accessible dental and medical equipment; staff-training failures.

Settlement obligations: facility audit and remediation under ADA Standards (28 C.F.R. § 36.406 / 2010 Standards); auxiliary-aids provision per § 36.303 and § 504 effective-communication requirements; equipment procurement (height-adjustable exam tables, wheelchair-accessible dental chairs, patient lifts where indicated); staff training; DOJ monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Sea Mar patients encountering current violations should report to DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov), HHS Office for Civil Rights (Section 504/Section 1557 enforcement, particularly relevant for FQHCs), Disability Rights Washington, and Washington State Human Rights Commission.
full_description_simple:
This is a deal between the U.S. Department of Justice and Sea Mar Community Health Centers from April 2025.

Sea Mar runs many community health centers across Washington state. The DOJ found that Sea Mar was not making its dental and medical clinics accessible. Wheelchair users couldn't get into exam rooms. Deaf patients didn't get sign language interpreters when they needed them.

Sea Mar agreed to:
- Fix the buildings.
- Provide free ASL interpreters for deaf patients.
- Buy accessible dental chairs and equipment.
- Train staff.

If you go to Sea Mar and you have a disability, and they're not following these rules, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without contemporaneous records. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint; HHS OCR (Section 504 + Section 1557, particularly relevant for FQHC); Disability Rights Washington (state P&A); Washington State Human Rights Commission.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without records:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free).
- HHS Office for Civil Rights (handles FQHC complaints).
- Disability Rights Washington.
- Washington State Human Rights Commission.

short_description_professional: April 2025 DOJ enforcement settlement under ADA Title III and Section 504 binding Sea Mar Community Health Centers (WA) to dental and medical-facility accessibility reforms. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: DOJ deal with Sea Mar from April 2025. Sea Mar (community health centers in Washington state) had to fix dental clinic accessibility — wheelchair access, communication for deaf patients, accessible exam chairs. If you've had problems at Sea Mar, you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: other healthcare providers (separate Title III/§ 504 enforcement); Sea Mar non-clinical programs; insurance/billing disputes; quality-of-care claims (state medical licensing boards, malpractice frameworks).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:

questions.prompt: What's your disability? (Service animal use, wheelchair, scooter, walker, other)
What did the Uber driver do? (Refused to pick you up, drove away after seeing your animal/device, charged improper fee, refused to stow your wheelchair, etc.)

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Active DOJ enforcement adds federal-government leverage on top of the 2016 settlement. Route to DOJ + in-app + NFB + state AG. For CA/NY/MA emphasize statutory damages. Distinguish from the NFB-CA settlement (both still active).

You can support DOJ's active case (and pursue your own remedies) if:
- You have a disability — service-animal use, wheelchair use (including stowable wheelchairs), or other disability covered by Title III.
- You used or tried to use Uber.
- A driver refused you, charged you improperly, or otherwise discriminated against you because of disability.

Reports of incidents from any state are useful for DOJ's case. State-law remedies are stronger in CA, NY, MA, and other states with statutory-damages frameworks.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who used or attempted to use Uber and encountered driver-level discrimination or improper surcharge assessment. Reports support DOJ's federal enforcement record; parallel state-law and private-remedy avenues remain available.

You can report if:

DOJ wants to hear from you. Your story helps the case.
evidence_guidance_professional: Contemporaneous: app screenshot capture; driver/vehicle identification; verbatim statement preservation; in-app report submission preserving carrier-side record. Post-incident: parallel DOJ + state AG + private-counsel complaint flow. Pattern evidence across incidents supports both the DOJ enforcement record and individual statutory-damages claims.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When it happens:

After:
- File the DOJ complaint within a few days.

full_description:
On September 11, 2025, the U.S. Department of Justice sued Uber Technologies, Inc. under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act. The case is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California (3:25-cv-07731-SK).

DOJ alleges that Uber:
- Routinely denies rides to passengers with service animals (often guide dogs).
- Routinely denies rides to passengers with stowable wheelchairs that can fit in standard Uber vehicles.
- Charges improper surcharges and cleaning fees related to disability.
- Has failed to adequately train drivers and enforce its own anti-discrimination policies.

This is a NEW case — separate from the 2016 NFB-CA v. Uber settlement (which is still in compliance phase; see related listing nfb-ca-v-uber-service-animals). DOJ's case takes the federal government's direct enforcement authority and applies it to Uber's ongoing pattern of driver-level discrimination.

What this means:
- If you've had an Uber driver refuse you because of your service animal, refuse to stow your wheelchair, or charge you a disability-related fee — you can support DOJ's case by reporting the incident.
- You can also (and should still) report through Uber's in-app system (required by the 2016 settlement) and via DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Litigation may take years. Compensatory damages may be awarded; settlement may result in additional enforcement mechanisms.

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- DOJ specifically on this case: civilrights.justice.gov.
- Uber's in-app reporting (still required by NFB-CA settlement).
- NFB testing program: testing@nfb.org.
- For California residents: California Civil Rights Department for Unruh Act statutory damages.
- For other strong-state-law jurisdictions: state AG civil-rights division.
full_description_professional:
United States v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 3:25-cv-07731-SK (N.D. Cal., filed Sept. 11, 2025), is an active DOJ Civil Rights Division enforcement action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq.

The complaint alleges systemic Title III violations: (i) driver-level denial of service to passengers with service animals as defined under 28 C.F.R. § 36.104 (impermissible defenses including allergies, fear, and religious objection per § 36.302(c)(2)); (ii) denial of service to passengers with stowable wheelchairs (failing § 36.302(a) reasonable-modification obligations); (iii) imposition of surcharges and cleaning fees prohibited by § 36.301(c); (iv) inadequate driver training and policy enforcement.

The action parallels but is distinct from NFB-CA v. Uber, 3:14-cv-04086 (settled 2016, ongoing compliance phase — see related listing). NFB-CA v. Uber created private-settlement enforcement mechanisms (in-app reporting, NFB testing, judicial monitor). The DOJ case invokes federal direct-enforcement authority and may yield compensatory damages, civil penalties, and additional injunctive relief beyond the 2016 settlement.

Disposition timeline: pleadings, discovery, and motion practice are ongoing as of mid-2026. Settlement is possible at any stage; trial likely 2027 or later if not settled.

Active enforcement avenues for affected passengers: DOJ complaint at ADA.gov and civilrights.justice.gov; Uber in-app reporting (settlement-required); NFB testing program; state AG and state civil-rights agencies for statutory-damages jurisdictions (CA Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA); private Title III + state-law counsel for individual claims.
full_description_simple:
The U.S. Department of Justice sued Uber in September 2025. DOJ is the federal government's top civil rights enforcer.

DOJ says Uber drivers:
- Refuse to give rides to blind people with guide dogs.
- Refuse to stow wheelchairs that easily fit in the car.
- Charge extra fees because of disability.

This is a new case. There's already an older settlement from 2016 (NFB-CA v. Uber, see related listing). Both are still active — the new DOJ case adds to the pressure on Uber.

What you should do if a driver refuses you:
- Report in the Uber app right away.
- File a DOJ complaint at ADA.gov.
- Tell the National Federation of the Blind testing program.
- For California: file with the CA Civil Rights Department — you can get $4,000 per incident under state law.
- For other states with strong laws (NY, MA, etc.): file with your state attorney general.

key_dates.Status: Active litigation; early phase

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Uber maintains backend trip data subject to DOJ subpoena. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov; civilrights.justice.gov); in-app reporting; NFB testing program; state AG; state civil-rights agencies; private counsel for parallel individual claims.

eligibility:

If you're experiencing this issue NOW at First Urology — meaning, the staff is still refusing to help you transfer, or insisting you bring your own equipment or family member — that's a settlement violation. You can:
- Report it to Disability Rights Advocates (the original class counsel) at dralegal.org.
- File a complaint with the DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.

eligibility_professional:
The settled class encompassed patients with qualifying mobility disabilities (42 U.S.C. § 12102) who sought medical or diagnostic services at any First Urology, P.S.C. facility in Kentucky or Indiana from the limitations period through final judgment. Class membership did not require completed treatment; refusal of service due to transfer-assistance denial was sufficient.

Post-settlement, the compliance posture creates parallel enforcement avenues for current barriers:
- Reporting to class counsel (Disability Rights Advocates) for monitoring purposes and potential contempt or settlement-enforcement proceedings.
- Administrative complaint to DOJ Civil Rights Division, Disability Rights Section under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502; DOJ retains enforcement authority over Title III compliance independent of the settlement.

eligibility_simple:
The lawsuit is settled. The settlement covers everyone who:
- Uses a wheelchair or other mobility device.
- Tried to go to a First Urology office in Kentucky or Indiana.
- Was told they had to bring their own help or equipment to transfer to an exam table.

If this is happening to you RIGHT NOW at a First Urology office, that means they are breaking the settlement. You can:

evidence_guidance_professional:
For documentation of current First Urology accessibility barriers:

Pre-visit:
- Submit a written accommodation request specifying transfer-assistance needs in advance of the appointment. Preserve the request and the response (or non-response).
- Inquire as to the availability of accessible medical equipment (height-adjustable exam tables, patient lifts, etc.) and document the response.

At visit:
- Document staff identity, time, location, the specific accommodation requested, and the verbatim refusal language.
- If accompanied, request that the accompanying person also memorialize the encounter independently.
- Preserve appointment confirmations, sign-in records, and any documentation indicating cancellation or denial of service.

Post-visit:
- Memorialize the incident in a contemporaneous written record (email-to-self with timestamp is sufficient).
- File administrative complaints in parallel: DOJ (ADA.gov), state civil-rights commission, and class counsel notice.

evidence_guidance_simple:
If you're going to a First Urology appointment and you're worried about transfer assistance:

Before the appointment:
- Call the office and ask: "If I need help transferring from my wheelchair to the exam table, can you help me, or do I need to bring someone?" Write down the answer and who said it.
- Bring a small notebook or use your phone to record the date and the staff name.

At the appointment:
- If you're refused transfer help, write down EXACTLY what was said (the words the staff used) before you leave.
- If you have to leave without being seen, ask for documentation of the refusal — even an appointment cancellation note.
- If you have a friend or family member with you, ask them to also write down what happened.

After:
- Email yourself the notes so they have a timestamp.
- Send a written complaint to the practice manager.
- File the DOJ complaint at ADA.gov. It is free.
full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against First Urology, the largest urological medical practice in the greater Louisville area, with about 20 locations across Kentucky and Indiana. Three wheelchair users — Elizabeth Fust, David Allgood, and Marcus Gray — together with the Center for Accessible Living, sued in August 2020 in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Kentucky. They were represented by Disability Rights Advocates.

The lawsuit challenged First Urology's written "Lifting Policy," which said the practice would not help patients transfer from wheelchairs or other mobility devices to exam tables or imaging equipment. Patients with mobility disabilities were told they had to bring their own equipment, or bring friends, family members, or attendants to help them transfer. If a patient didn't bring their own help, First Urology refused treatment. Elizabeth Fust was refused diagnostic imaging entirely at all 20 locations and told to go to a hospital instead.

The U.S. Department of Justice opened a parallel investigation and reached a settlement: First Urology paid $60,000 and agreed to change the policy, train its staff, and provide proper transfer equipment and assistance at all its locations. The private class action ran in parallel.

The case is now in compliance phase — the policy is gone, but advocates continue to monitor whether First Urology actually provides the equipment and assistance the settlement requires.
full_description_professional:
Fust, et al. v. First Urology, P.S.C., 3:20-cv-00562 (W.D. Ky., filed Aug. 12, 2020), is a putative class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, and the Kentucky Civil Rights Act. Plaintiffs Elizabeth Fust, David Allgood, and Marcus Gray, joined by the Center for Accessible Living, were represented by Disability Rights Advocates. The action targeted First Urology's written "Lifting Policy," which provided that patients requiring assistance to transfer from mobility devices to examination tables or diagnostic equipment must supply their own assistive devices or accompanying personnel; absent such provision, First Urology refused treatment.

A parallel DOJ enforcement action under the United States Attorney for the Western District of Kentucky resulted in a settlement requiring First Urology to: (i) pay $60,000; (ii) rescind the Lifting Policy in writing; (iii) procure accessible medical equipment (including height-adjustable exam tables and patient lifts where indicated) at its facilities; (iv) train staff on transfer assistance and ADA Title III obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(a)'s reasonable modification mandate; and (v) institute monitoring and reporting obligations.

The matter is in ongoing compliance phase. Patients encountering current refusals of transfer assistance at First Urology facilities should report violations to DRA and consider DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502. The case is significant precedent for analogous medical-practice accessibility enforcement: refusal of transfer assistance is a Title III "policy or procedure" requiring reasonable modification, not a permissible safety exception.
full_description_simple:
This case was a class-action lawsuit against First Urology — a urology practice with about 20 offices in Kentucky and Indiana. It is now settled.

Three patients who use wheelchairs sued First Urology in 2020. The practice had a written policy that said staff would not help patients move from their wheelchair to an exam table or to an imaging machine. Patients had to bring their own lifting equipment, or bring a family member or friend to help them. If they didn't, the practice would not treat them.

One patient, Elizabeth Fust, was told to go to a hospital because no First Urology office would help her get onto a table for her imaging test.

The U.S. Department of Justice also got involved. First Urology agreed to change the policy and pay $60,000. They had to train staff and buy the right equipment to help patients with disabilities transfer safely.

Right now the case is being monitored to make sure First Urology is actually following the new rules. If you go to a First Urology office and they refuse to help you transfer to an exam table, that is exactly the behavior the settlement banned. You can report it.
no_documentation_path_professional:
Absence of contemporaneous documentation does not foreclose reporting. The settlement created an ongoing compliance regime that responds to current incident reports, not solely retrospective claims.

Class members and current First Urology patients should consider:
- Direct contact with Disability Rights Advocates (dralegal.org) regarding any post-settlement refusals. DRA retains an interest in settlement enforcement and may pursue contempt or supplementary proceedings.
- DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502, which may proceed regardless of the private settlement.
- Documenting prospective incidents contemporaneously: appointment confirmations, staff statements (preserving as much verbatim as possible), location, date, and any alternative care sought.
- Pre-suit demand letters to First Urology referencing the settlement and the specific incident, which may resolve issues without administrative escalation.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You don't need a lot of paperwork to report this. If a First Urology office refused to help you transfer to an exam table today, last week, or last month — that's enough to report.

You can:
- Call or write to Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org. They know the case and will follow up.
- File a free DOJ complaint at ADA.gov. It takes about 30 minutes online.
- File with the Kentucky Commission on Human Rights or Indiana Civil Rights Commission.

Going forward, even just writing yourself an email about what happened at the appointment (date, office, what was said) creates a record. Don't worry about doing it perfectly.
short_description: Settled class action and parallel DOJ enforcement against First Urology over a "Lifting Policy" that refused to help patients with mobility disabilities transfer from wheelchairs to exam tables or imaging equipment. DOJ obtained $60,000 settlement plus policy and training reforms.
short_description_professional: Class action under Title III ADA, Section 504, and Kentucky Civil Rights Act resolved alongside parallel DOJ enforcement (W.D. Ky.) targeting First Urology's "Lifting Policy" — a written policy refusing transfer assistance from wheelchairs to exam tables and diagnostic equipment. DOJ resolution: $60,000 payment plus injunctive policy, equipment, and training reforms across all First Urology locations in Kentucky and Indiana.
short_description_simple: First Urology used to refuse to help wheelchair users get onto exam tables. Patients sued, the DOJ stepped in, and the policy is now banned at all 20 of First Urology's locations.
what_this_is_not_professional:
Scope limitations of this action:
- The settlement binds First Urology, P.S.C. only. Other medical practices with analogous transfer-assistance refusals require separate enforcement, though the Fust precedent and DOJ settlement template inform such efforts.
- The action addresses transfer assistance and the related equipment-provision and training obligations. Other Title III barriers at First Urology — facility access under 28 C.F.R. § 36.406, effective-communication failures under § 36.303, denial of service to service-animal users under § 36.302(c) — are not pleaded in this settlement and require separate enforcement.
- The DOJ resolution's $60,000 payment was directed to the United States; individual class-member damages were not the focus. Section 504 may provide for individualized compensatory remedies in cases of deliberate indifference, but litigating such claims requires separate action.
- The settlement does not preempt parallel actions under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act (which incorporates Section 504 for federally funded healthcare entities), state common-law negligence theories, or analogous state public-accommodation claims.
what_this_is_not_simple:
A few things this case does NOT cover:
- Medical practices other than First Urology. Other practices may have similar issues, but the settlement only binds First Urology.
- Other accessibility issues at First Urology — like a building with no ramp, a website that doesn't work with screen readers, or appointment scheduling problems. Those could be ADA issues, but this specific case is about transfer assistance.
- Money damages for past refusals. The settlement was about changing the policy, not paying out individual patients.
- Hospital admissions or non-urology care. The case is about urology offices.

If your medical-access issue is something else, Ada can help you find the right path.

full_description:
This is a class-action lawsuit against the San Diego County Sheriff's Department over conditions and accessibility in San Diego County jails. Filed in March 2020 in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of California.

The ADA Title II and Section 504 claims — focused on accessibility for prisoners with mobility, sensory, intellectual, and mental health disabilities — were settled in August 2025. The settlement requires:
- Audit and remediation of physical access at all SDCSO-operated jail facilities.
- Provision of accessible programming, including for prisoners with mobility, vision, hearing, and intellectual disabilities.
- Qualified ASL interpreters for deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners.
- Reasonable modifications to disciplinary, classification, and programming systems.
- Training for custody and program staff.
- Ongoing monitoring.

Other claims in the case — including Eighth Amendment claims about medical care, mental health treatment, and overall conditions — remain in active litigation as of mid-2026.

If you or a family member is currently in San Diego County jail and isn't getting required accommodations under the settlement, that's a settlement-violation report worth making. Contact Disability Rights California or Disability Rights Advocates (who are involved in this matter).
full_description_professional:
Dunsmore, et al. v. San Diego County Sheriff's Department, 3:20-cv-00406-AJB-DDL (S.D. Cal., filed March 2, 2020), is a partially settled class action. The ADA settlement implements: facility audit and remediation per ADA Standards and 28 C.F.R. § 35.151; programmatic accessibility under § 35.130(b)(7) for disciplinary, classification, educational, vocational, and treatment programming; qualified-interpreter provision for deaf prisoners under § 35.160; reasonable modifications; staff training; ongoing monitoring.

Compliance phase for the ADA portion ongoing. Class counsel includes Disability Rights California and Disability Rights Advocates. Current non-compliance may be reported to class counsel, DOJ Civil Rights Division under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170, California Civil Rights Department, and California Attorney General's Office.

full_description:
State and local government websites and online services — DMV, courts, benefits portals (SNAP, Medicaid, unemployment), tax systems, voter registration, and dozens of other services — are required by federal law to be accessible.

In April 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice finalized a rule under ADA Title II that explicitly requires state and local governments to make their web content and mobile apps conform to WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards. The compliance deadlines are:
- April 24, 2026: All states and government entities serving populations of 50,000 or more.
- April 26, 2027: Smaller entities (under 50,000 population) and special-purpose districts.

If you're a person with a disability and you can't use a state or local government website — your screen reader can't read the form, the captcha blocks you, the PDF is image-only, the video has no captions, the buttons aren't labeled, the mobile app crashes with VoiceOver — that's a Title II violation. The largest jurisdictions are already required to comply.

Report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free).
- The state attorney general's civil rights division.
- The state Protection & Advocacy entity (every state has one).
- For DMVs and courts specifically, the state disability advocacy office.
full_description_professional:
DOJ's final rule at 28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq., effective April 24, 2024 (compliance deadlines April 24, 2026 for entities serving populations of 50,000+ and all states; April 26, 2027 for entities under 50,000 and special purpose districts), explicitly requires state and local government web content and mobile apps to conform to WCAG 2.1 AA.

Substantive requirements: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for all web content under entity control; mobile-application accessibility; remediation of legacy content; auxiliary-aids provision for assistive-technology users; ongoing monitoring and complaint-resolution procedures.

The rule applies to all Title II covered entities (state and local governments and their instrumentalities). Implementation will trigger sustained private litigation and DOJ enforcement; early enforcement priorities include voter-facing services, public benefits portals, and DMV systems.

Reporting and enforcement: DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170; state attorney general civil-rights divisions; state Protection & Advocacy entities; private Title II actions for injunctive relief and fees under 42 U.S.C. § 12205. For voter-facing services, parallel HAVA enforcement is available.
full_description_simple:
Government websites need to work for everyone — including people with disabilities. State DMVs, courts, benefits portals, and other government websites have to be accessible by law.

In 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice made this an official, enforceable rule with deadlines:
- April 2026: All states and any local government serving 50,000+ people must comply.
- April 2027: Smaller cities and counties must comply.

If you couldn't use a government website because you're blind, low vision, deaf, or have a learning or motor disability — that's a violation.

Examples of violations:
- DMV renewal form doesn't work with screen readers.
- Court e-filing system has unlabeled buttons.
- Unemployment portal video has no captions.
- Benefits application is an image PDF you can't read.
- State tax website crashes with VoiceOver.

Report it:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Your state attorney general.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office (Google "[your state] Protection and Advocacy").

key_dates.Status: Active enforcement; April 2026 deadline now in effect for large entities

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without documentation. Channels: DOJ § 35.170 administrative complaint; state AG civil-rights division; state P&A; agency internal complaint process (creates notice); private Title II counsel for litigation pursuit.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Even without screenshots:

- Your state attorney general.
- Your state P&A (Protection & Advocacy) office.
- The agency itself (file a complaint internally too — it creates a record).
short_description: DMV websites, benefits portals, court systems, or state agency online services that block access for screen reader users. April 2026 DOJ deadline now active for the largest jurisdictions.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II web accessibility — DOJ's April 24, 2024 final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) sets enforceable WCAG 2.1 AA compliance deadlines for state and local government digital services. April 24, 2026 deadline applies to entities with populations of 50,000+ (and all states); April 26, 2027 for entities under 50,000.
short_description_simple: The DMV, court system, benefits portal, or state agency website you needed to use doesn't work with your screen reader. DOJ has a NEW rule (April 2026 deadline for large jurisdictions) requiring state and local government websites to be accessible.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: federal government digital services (29 U.S.C. § 794d / Section 508 framework, GSA/OPM enforcement); private-business sites (Title III separate); non-US government services; phone-line accessibility (separate Title II + TRS framework).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Federal government websites (different framework: Section 508).
- Private business websites (Title III is separate).
- Non-US government websites.
- Phone-only services that don't involve a website (separate Title II claims).

You don't need to be a registered disability services student. The institutional obligation exists independent of registration. (Registering with disability services strengthens your case for accommodation, but isn't required to file an external complaint.)
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to current, prospective, and recent students with qualifying disabilities at any US institution of higher education subject to Title II, Title III, or § 504 jurisdiction. Disability-services-office registration is not a prerequisite for external complaint filing or for class membership in private litigation, though registration may be relevant to individualized accommodation-history claims.

full_description:
College and graduate-school online learning has barriers for students with disabilities everywhere. Federal law requires accessibility, and DOJ has been increasingly active in enforcement.

Common violations:
- Learning management systems (Canvas, Blackboard, Moodle, D2L, etc.) with unlabeled buttons, broken keyboard navigation, or screen-reader failures.
- Course videos without captions or transcripts.
- Course PDFs that are scanned-image-only (unreadable by screen readers).
- Online proctoring software (Proctorio, ProctorU, Honorlock, etc.) that requires features that don't work for blind or motor-impaired students, OR that flags disability-related behavior as "cheating."
- Online testing platforms with timing requirements that don't accommodate extended time accommodations.
- STEM-specific accessibility failures (math equations as images, inaccessible lab simulations, inaccessible Jupyter notebooks).
- Communication channels (Zoom without captions, Slack inaccessible, etc.).

The legal framework:
- Public colleges and universities: Title II (state actor) + Section 504 (federal funding).
- Private colleges and universities: Title III (place of public accommodation) + Section 504 (almost all receive federal funding via student aid).
- DOJ 2023 Dear Colleague Letter specifically addressed higher-education digital accessibility.
- DOJ 2024 Title II web rule applies to public institutions with WCAG 2.1 AA compliance deadlines.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights enforces Section 504 across all federally funded schools.

Where to report:
- Your school's disability services office (file an internal complaint — creates a record).
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights (Section 504, all federally funded schools).
- The U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights.
- State Protection & Advocacy.
- Private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN) for systemic issues.
full_description_professional:
Higher-education digital accessibility is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for public institutions; Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for private institutions of public accommodation; Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794 and 34 C.F.R. Part 104) for all federally funded institutions; Section 1557 of the ACA (42 U.S.C. § 18116) for healthcare-program intersection.

The DOJ 2023 Dear Colleague Letter on Digital Accessibility in Higher Education provides interpretive guidance. The April 2024 DOJ Title II final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) imposes specific WCAG 2.1 AA compliance deadlines on public institutions: April 24, 2026 for large entities (50,000+ population served, and all state institutions); April 26, 2027 for smaller entities.

Common substantive issues litigated: LMS accessibility (NFB v. UC Berkeley, NFB v. MIT, et seq.); auto-captioning quality and live captioning provision; image-based PDFs and inaccessible STEM content; proctoring-software discrimination (post-2020 surge of complaints); testing-time-accommodation enforcement.

Enforcement avenues: DOJ Civil Rights Division (ADA.gov, 28 C.F.R. § 35.170); HHS OCR (§ 504 enforcement, ed.hhs.gov complaint portal); Department of Education OCR (education-specific § 504 + Title II enforcement, ed.gov/ocr); state Protection & Advocacy entities; private rights of action for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees under 42 U.S.C. § 12205 and 29 U.S.C. § 794a(b).
full_description_simple:
Colleges and universities use computer tools to teach. Some of these tools don't work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The Canvas or Blackboard site doesn't work with your screen reader.
- The professor's videos have no captions.
- The course PDFs are pictures of text — your screen reader can't read them.
- The online test software won't let you use your assistive technology, or marks you as a cheater because of your disability.
- Math equations are images.
- Zoom classes don't have captions.

The law:
- Public colleges (state schools): must comply with the ADA AND Section 504.
- Private colleges: also must comply with the ADA and Section 504 (because almost all take federal money).

Where to report:
- Your school's disability services office — file an internal complaint first. It creates a record.
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights — they handle education-specific complaints.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights (also covers federally funded schools).
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office.

key_dates.Status: Active enforcement; deadlines now in effect for large public institutions

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: ED OCR (specifically for education-sector complaints; substantially more responsive than DOJ for higher-ed-specific issues); DOJ (parallel); HHS OCR; state P&A; private disability-rights counsel (DRA, NDRN).
no_documentation_path_simple:
You don't need a lot of paperwork. Most schools track accommodation requests internally — they can be subpoenaed.

Where to start:
- The DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The U.S. Department of Education OCR (handles college accessibility complaints): ed.gov/ocr.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office.
- Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org.
short_description: Inaccessible LMS, course materials, videos without captions, or online testing platforms at colleges and universities.
short_description_professional: ADA Title II (public colleges) / Title III (private colleges) + Section 504 governance of higher-education digital accessibility. DOJ's 2023 Dear Colleague Letter and the 2024 DOJ Title II web rule (April 2026 deadline for large public institutions) frame enforcement. Common violations: inaccessible LMS, uncaptioned video, inaccessible proctoring software, image-only PDF course materials, inaccessible online testing platforms.
short_description_simple: Your college or university uses online tools — learning management systems (Canvas, Blackboard), video lectures, online tests, course PDFs — that don't work for people with disabilities. This is illegal under federal law. Report it to DOJ AND your school's disability services office.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: campus physical accessibility (separate Title II/III); employment by institution (Title I + state employment-discrimination law); quality-of-instruction non-disability claims (institutional grievance processes); admissions (separate framework, particularly graduate/professional school + LSAT/MCAT/etc.); K-12 (separate IDEA framework + Title II); for-profit technical schools may have separate regulatory framework.
what_this_is_not_simple:

- Employment discrimination by the school (separate Title I + state employment-law claims).

- Admissions discrimination (separate framework; particularly for graduate/medical schools where standardized testing is involved).
- K-12 schools (see k12-digital-access).
- Federal trade/technical schools (different framework).

- OR you're an adult student in a public K-12 setting.
- OR you're a parent/guardian with a disability whose child's school system isn't accessible to YOU.
- The student (or parent) has a disability — any kind.
- A digital tool used by the school district or required by the curriculum has accessibility barriers.

Coverage extends to:
- Public school districts (state or local).
- Charter schools.
- For private schools: Title II doesn't apply, but Section 504 does if they receive federal funding (most do for various programs). Title III applies if they're a place of public accommodation.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to: K-12 students with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102, 29 U.S.C. § 705(9)(B), or 20 U.S.C. § 1401(3); parents/guardians with qualifying disabilities encountering inaccessible district-facing systems; and prospective students (e.g., enrollment-system accessibility). Title II applies to public schools and charter schools as state actors; Section 504 applies to all federally funded institutions; IDEA's overlay applies to students with IEPs and 504 plans.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:

- OR you're a parent with a disability.

full_description:
Public school districts and charter schools are state actors under ADA Title II and recipients of federal funding under Section 504 — both of which require digital accessibility. The DOJ April 2024 Title II final rule explicitly applies to school districts with WCAG 2.1 AA conformance deadlines (April 24, 2026 for districts serving 50,000+ residents in their jurisdiction).

Common K-12 digital accessibility violations:
- District websites with inaccessible online enrollment, lunch payments, transportation forms, school calendars.
- Parent/guardian portals (PowerSchool, Skyward, Infinite Campus, etc.) that don't work with screen readers or have unlabeled forms.
- Learning platforms (Google Classroom, Schoology, Seesaw, Clever, Canvas K-12) with accessibility failures.
- Homework systems (IXL, Khan Academy, ALEKS, etc.) that don't work with assistive technology.
- Video lessons without captions or transcripts (especially relevant for deaf/HoH students).
- Scanned-image PDF assignments unreadable by screen readers.
- Online testing platforms (state assessments, district benchmarks) without accommodation supports.
- Communication apps (ClassDojo, Remind, Bloomz) inaccessible to deaf or low-vision parents.

The legal framework:
- ADA Title II + Section 504 govern accessibility for students AND parents/guardians with disabilities.
- IDEA (Individuals with Disabilities Education Act) creates additional obligations for students with IEPs and 504 plans, including accessible instructional materials.
- AIM/AEM (Accessible Educational Materials) standards apply for students with print disabilities.

Where to report:
- The district's 504 coordinator or special education office (file internally first).
- U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights (the primary K-12 enforcer): ed.gov/ocr.
- DOJ Civil Rights Division.
- State Department of Education.
- State Protection & Advocacy.
full_description_professional:
K-12 digital accessibility is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35); Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794, 34 C.F.R. Part 104); IDEA (20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq., 34 C.F.R. Part 300); and the Accessible Educational Materials provisions under IDEA. The DOJ April 2024 Title II final rule (28 C.F.R. § 35.200 et seq.) explicitly extends WCAG 2.1 AA compliance obligations to public school districts with deadlines of April 24, 2026 (large entities) and April 26, 2027 (smaller entities).

Substantive obligations span: (i) general public-facing accessibility for parents/community; (ii) student-facing instructional content accessibility, with augmented obligations under IDEA's FAPE (Free Appropriate Public Education) standard and Section 504's "equal opportunity" standard; (iii) AIM/AEM provision for students with print disabilities under IDEA's NIMAS framework; (iv) communication-access for deaf parents and students; (v) accommodation-supported online testing.

Enforcement avenues: institutional § 504 coordinator complaint (creates notice, exhausts administrative remedies); ED OCR (primary K-12 enforcement; ed.gov/ocr); DOJ Civil Rights Division (28 C.F.R. § 35.170); state DOE compliance offices; state P&A entities; private § 504 actions and IDEA Due Process Hearings for individual relief.
full_description_simple:
Schools and school districts have to make their websites and computer tools accessible to kids with disabilities AND parents with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The school website parent portal doesn't work with your screen reader.
- Your child's learning platform (Google Classroom, Schoology, etc.) won't work with their assistive technology.
- Homework apps (IXL, Khan Academy, etc.) aren't accessible.
- Videos in class have no captions for a deaf student.
- The PDF homework is a picture you can't read.
- Online testing for state tests doesn't accommodate disabilities.
- Class apps (ClassDojo, Remind) don't work for blind parents.

April 2026 deadline: large school districts now have to comply with accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1 AA). Smaller districts have until April 2027.

Where to report:
- The school district's 504 coordinator or special education office FIRST. This creates a record.
- U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights at ed.gov/ocr — this is the main place for school complaints.
- Your state Department of Education.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy office.

Channels: institutional § 504 coordinator (exhausts administrative remedies); ED OCR (primary K-12 enforcement); DOJ § 35.170; state DOE; state P&A; COPAA referral for parent-advocate connection; private § 504 / IDEA Due Process counsel for individualized relief.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Where to report without records:

- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Your state Protection & Advocacy.
- Council of Parent Attorneys and Advocates (COPAA) at copaa.org for parent-advocate support.

short_description_professional: ADA Title II + Section 504 + IDEA framework for K-12 digital accessibility. DOJ's April 2024 Title II final rule (WCAG 2.1 AA, April 2026 deadline for large entities) explicitly covers public school districts. Common violations: inaccessible district websites, student/parent portals, learning platforms (Google Classroom, Schoology, Seesaw, etc.), homework systems, and online testing.
