full_description:
Self-service kiosks are increasingly common — and increasingly inaccessible to people with disabilities. ADA Title III requires these devices to be usable by all customers, including those who are blind, low-vision, deaf, hard-of-hearing, or have motor impairments.

Common kiosk accessibility violations:
- Touchscreen-only interfaces with no tactile keypad or audio output (blocks blind users).
- Screen text too small or low contrast (blocks low-vision users).
- Visual-only confirmations (blocks deaf-blind users).
- No headphone jack for audio output (privacy issue for blind users).
- Kiosks mounted too high for wheelchair users.
- Required tasks (payment, signature, PIN entry) that can't be done by users with motor or sensory disabilities without disclosing private information to a third party.

Specific contexts where this matters:
- Pharmacy POS terminals: blind users forced to disclose prescriptions, payment info, or signatures verbally to clerks.
- Restaurant ordering kiosks (McDonald's, etc.): forced to depend on staff to order.
- Airport check-in: dependence on agents who may not be available.
- Hospital and clinic check-in: confidential medical info exposed.
- Government service kiosks: similar problems.
- Retail self-checkout: similar issues with payment and SKU lookup.

The legal framework:
- ADA Title III governs places of public accommodation, which includes pharmacies, restaurants, retail, and most other settings using kiosks.
- ADA Title II governs government-operated kiosks (DMV, courts, etc.).
- Section 508 governs federal-government kiosks.
- DOJ has not yet issued kiosk-specific regulations, but has signaled enforcement intent.

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- The business directly (creates notice).
- State attorney general consumer-protection or civil-rights division.
- Disability-rights legal organizations (DRA, NFB, NDRN, ACB).
full_description_professional:
Self-service kiosk accessibility is governed primarily by ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for public-accommodation operators and Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for government-operated devices. Section 508 (29 U.S.C. § 794d) governs federal-government kiosks specifically.

Substantive requirements derive from Title III's effective-communication mandate (28 C.F.R. § 36.303), policy-and-procedure modification obligation (§ 36.302(a)), and reasonable-accommodation mandate. The 2010 ADA Standards for Accessible Design include some kiosk-specific provisions (Section 707 for ATMs; analogous principles applied to other transaction machines).

Litigation has established the pattern: NFB v. Caesar's Entertainment (gambling-floor kiosks); NFB v. Apple Store (self-checkout); multiple settlements with pharmacy chains (CVS, Walgreens point-of-sale equipment); restaurant chain settlements (Panera Bread kiosk accessibility); airport check-in litigation. DOJ has signaled increased enforcement priority for kiosk accessibility.

Reporting and enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint; state AG consumer-protection/civil-rights enforcement; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; coordination with NFB, ACB, NDRN, DRA for systemic litigation.
full_description_simple:
Self-service kiosks — the screens you use to order food, check in at the airport, pay at the pharmacy, or do other things — often don't work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The pharmacy kiosk needs you to read a touch screen — you can't if you're blind.
- The McDonald's ordering kiosk has tiny text — you can't read it with low vision.
- The airport check-in kiosk needs you to see what's on the screen — there's no audio.
- The kiosk is too high for your wheelchair.
- The kiosk needs you to type in a PIN or password — you can't hide it from people watching.

The law:
- The ADA says public-accommodation kiosks must be usable by people with disabilities.

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Tell the business directly — get the manager's name.
- Your state attorney general.
- Disability rights organizations like the National Federation of the Blind (nfb.org).

key_dates.Notable litigation: NFB v. Caesar's (settled), NFB v. Apple Store (settled), pharmacy POS settlements
key_dates.Status: Active individual + systemic litigation; no comprehensive DOJ kiosk rule yet

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: DOJ § 36.502 administrative complaint; business corporate accessibility office (creates notice); state AG civil-rights/consumer-protection; NFB, ACB, NDRN, DRA for systemic litigation; private Title III counsel.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free).
- The business's corporate accessibility office (most large chains have one).
- Your state attorney general.
- The National Federation of the Blind: nfb.org.
- The American Council of the Blind: acb.org.
short_description: Self-service kiosks at pharmacies, restaurants, airports, or government offices that aren't usable by blind, low-vision, or deaf customers.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III kiosk accessibility. Common targets: pharmacy POS terminals (CVS, Walgreens, etc.), restaurant ordering kiosks (McDonald's, fast-casual), airport check-in kiosks, hospital and clinic check-in, government-service kiosks, retail self-checkout. Litigation is largely individual but pattern is established (e.g., NFB v. Caesar's, settlement re point-of-sale equipment). DOJ has signaled increased enforcement.
short_description_simple: Self-service kiosks — at pharmacies, restaurants, airports, hospitals, government offices, retail stores, and more — often can't be used by blind, low-vision, or deaf customers. This is illegal under the ADA. Report violations.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: ATMs (Section 707 ADA Standards-specific framework, well-established remedies); vending machines (separate framework with limited ADA application); personal devices (Title III nexus theory, separate enforcement context); employer-only kiosks (Title I + state employment-discrimination law).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- ATMs (separate ADA Standards § 707 framework; many existing remedies).
- Vending machines (different framework).
- Personal devices like phones or tablets (Title III nexus theory may apply, see mobile-app-screen-reader).
- Workplace kiosks for employees only (Title I employment framework).

questions.prompt: Do you use a wheelchair, walker, scooter, or other mobility device to get around?
Did the barrier you encountered happen on a sidewalk or street corner in the city of Philadelphia (not the suburbs)?
What was the problem? Missing curb ramp, broken ramp, too steep, missing the bumpy yellow strip, or other?
questions.purpose: Identifies the specific Title II violation.

questions.prompt: What did you have to do because of it? Go into the road, go around the block, give up, fall, almost fall?
Do you have a photo of the barrier?
Have you previously called Philadelphia 311 about this intersection?
questions.purpose: Establishes notice; patterns of city non-responsiveness are independently actionable.

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Ask one at a time. If barrier was outside Philadelphia city limits, redirect — different county/township obligations. For barriers within scope, validate and route to 311 + Liberty Resources + DOJ as multiple parallel reporting channels. Encourage photo documentation. If user has tried 311 without response, that's itself important — pattern of city unresponsiveness is independently actionable under the settlement.

documentation_required_professional:
Documentation supporting non-compliance reports:
- Precise location (intersection cross-streets or GPS coordinates).
- Photographic documentation of the ramp condition (missing ramp, slope, detectable warnings, path-of-travel alignment, surface condition).
- Date and time of encounter.
- Description of impact: rerouting required, ingress to vehicular traffic, physical injury, alternative-route distance and time burden.
- Any prior city complaints filed regarding the same intersection (city service requests via 311 establish notice).
documentation_required_simple:
What helps:
- The exact street corner address (or nearest cross-streets).
- A photo showing the missing ramp, broken ramp, or improper ramp.
- The date you encountered it.
- Notes about how it stopped you — went into the road, had to go around the block, fell or almost fell, etc.

A phone photo with the date is enough. You don't need formal documentation.
eligibility:
The settlement protects people with mobility disabilities in Philadelphia and visitors who experience curb-ramp barriers anywhere in the city.

You can report a problem if:
- You use a wheelchair, walker, scooter, cane, or other mobility device.
- You encountered a Philadelphia city sidewalk where a curb ramp was missing, broken, too steep, or otherwise inaccessible.
- The barrier kept you from crossing the street, or forced you into the road, or stranded you on a block.

You don't need to have been part of the original class. The settlement creates ongoing citywide obligations.

This case is about Philadelphia City sidewalks. It does NOT cover SEPTA or transit-related accessibility, private property, or sidewalks in suburban townships.
eligibility_professional:
The settlement creates ongoing rights for persons with qualifying mobility disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 who encounter inaccessible pedestrian rights-of-way maintained by the City of Philadelphia.

Scope limitations:
- City-maintained pedestrian rights-of-way only. SEPTA-owned or -maintained facilities are subject to separate Title II claims.
- Excludes private-property pedestrian features. State and federal property within Philadelphia (federal courthouses, state buildings) are subject to separate Title II/Section 504 enforcement.

Reporting and enforcement:
- Plaintiffs' counsel (Liberty Resources): contact for systemic violation reporting.
- Philadelphia Department of Streets: city-level complaint process.
- DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
- Direct settlement enforcement in E.D. Pa. for substantial systemic non-compliance.
eligibility_simple:
You can report a problem in Philadelphia if:
- You use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or other mobility device.
- You ran into a Philadelphia city sidewalk where the curb ramp was missing, broken, or too steep.
- The problem kept you from crossing safely.

It does NOT cover:
- SEPTA buses, trains, or stations.
- Sidewalks in townships outside Philadelphia.
- Private property sidewalks.
evidence_guidance_professional:
For prospective documentation of pedestrian-access barriers:

Field documentation:
- Photographic capture from multiple angles establishing ramp absence, slope deficiency (visually evident steepness), detectable-warning failures (missing or non-compliant truncated domes), or path-of-travel misalignment.
- GPS coordinates or precise intersection identification.
- Where measurable, ramp dimensions, slope percentages, and surface condition.

Reporting:
- File a 311 service request to establish city notice and create administrative record.
- Submit to plaintiffs' counsel for inclusion in settlement-compliance reports.
- Where systemic non-compliance is evident on a corridor or in a neighborhood, document the pattern and report jointly.

Class-level enforcement support:
- Maintain a tracker of repeatedly reported intersections that remain unremediated.
- Document any city responses to 311 requests; pattern of unresponsiveness or inadequate responses is independently actionable under the settlement.
evidence_guidance_simple:
If you're documenting curb ramp problems in Philadelphia:

When you encounter a problem:
- Take a photo. A few photos showing the corner from different angles is even better.
- Note the cross streets.
- Try to capture: the missing ramp, the slope (if it's too steep), whether there's a yellow bumpy strip at the bottom, whether the ramp lines up with the crosswalk.

After:
- Email yourself the photos so they're timestamped.
- Submit a 311 request to the city.
- Send the photo to Liberty Resources at libertyresources.org.

For patterns:
- If you're tracking multiple problem intersections in your neighborhood, keep a list. Patterns help systemic enforcement.
full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of Philadelphia, filed by Liberty Resources, Inc. (a Philadelphia center for independent living) in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania in August 2019.

The lawsuit challenged Philadelphia's decades-long failure to install ADA-compliant curb ramps at intersections and to maintain existing ramps when they fell into disrepair. People who use wheelchairs, walkers, scooters, or have other mobility disabilities were effectively cut off from the public sidewalk network — unable to cross streets safely, forced into the roadway with vehicle traffic, or stuck on a block with no way out.

The case was settled and Philadelphia agreed to a remediation schedule covering the entire city, with priority routes, deadlines, and reporting obligations to plaintiffs and the court. The settlement is in compliance phase — meaning, the obligations are in place, the schedule is running, and reports of non-compliance can trigger enforcement.

If you encounter a missing curb ramp, a curb ramp that doesn't meet ADA Standards (too steep, no detectable warning, no flush transition, located out of the line of travel), or a damaged ramp that should have been repaired, you can report it to Liberty Resources or directly to Philadelphia.
full_description_professional:
Liberty Resources, Inc. v. City of Philadelphia (E.D. Pa., filed Aug. 26, 2019), is a settled class action under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing the City's programmatic failure to provide accessible curb ramps and maintain them in compliance with ADA Standards for Accessible Design (§§ 4.7 / 406, depending on applicable design epoch) and 28 C.F.R. § 35.151(i).

The settlement binds the City to a court-supervised remediation schedule covering installation of new ramps at uncompleted intersections and replacement/repair of non-compliant or deteriorated ramps. The schedule incorporates priority routing, periodic reporting obligations, and an ongoing private right to enforce compliance.

The matter remains in compliance phase. Reports of non-compliance — including missing ramps, ramps failing applicable slope/transition/detectable-warning requirements, mislocated ramps not aligned with the pedestrian path of travel, or ramps left unrepaired beyond scheduled maintenance windows — may be made to plaintiffs' counsel (Liberty Resources) and to the Philadelphia Department of Streets. DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170 are also available.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against the City of Philadelphia. It is now settled.

A group called Liberty Resources sued the city in 2019. They said Philadelphia has not put curb ramps at most street corners. Without ramps, people who use wheelchairs, walkers, or scooters cannot cross the street safely. They have to go into the road with cars, or they cannot leave their block at all.

The city agreed to fix this. They have a schedule for putting in ramps and fixing broken ones across the whole city.

If you live in or visit Philadelphia and you find:
- A street corner with NO ramp
- A ramp that's too steep, broken, or missing the bumpy yellow strip at the bottom
- A ramp in the wrong place (not where the crosswalk is)

— that may be a violation. You can report it to Liberty Resources or to the city.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, ongoing monitoring

no_documentation_path_professional:
Documentary records are not required for reporting. Plaintiffs and the city accept narrative reports of non-compliance.

Reporting channels:
- Philadelphia 311 (city service request system).
- Plaintiffs' counsel (Liberty Resources).
- DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint (ADA.gov).
- Class counsel direct contact for systemic pattern reporting.
- Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission for parallel state-law claims.
- Prospective documentation: contemporaneous photographic capture of barriers strengthens systemic enforcement and individual claims.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You don't need much. If you remember the intersection (even roughly), that's enough to file a complaint. The city or Liberty Resources can investigate from there.

Ways to report:
- Call Philadelphia 311 or use the city app to file a sidewalk/curb ramp request.
- Call Liberty Resources at libertyresources.org.
- File a free complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice at ADA.gov.
- Tweet at the Philadelphia Department of Streets — public pressure helps.

Going forward, take photos when you see broken ramps. It's easier to report later if you have a photo to attach.
short_description: Settled class action; Philadelphia agreed to install and repair curb ramps citywide on a schedule. Compliance phase — report missing or non-compliant curb ramps.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under ADA Title II and Section 504 requiring City of Philadelphia to install and remediate curb ramps citywide on a court-supervised schedule. Compliance phase; report Title II violations to plaintiffs' counsel and city.
short_description_simple: Settled in 2019. Philadelphia must fix curb ramps citywide. If you find a corner without a ramp, with a broken ramp, or with a too-steep slope — you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional:
Scope limitations:
- Bound only the City of Philadelphia for its pedestrian rights-of-way curb-ramp obligations. SEPTA, PennDOT, federal facilities, and private property are outside scope.
- Limited to curb-ramp installation and remediation. Other Title II/§504 obligations (programmatic accessibility, communication access, employment, etc.) require separate enforcement.
- Compliance-phase, not damages. Section 504 may provide individual compensatory remedies in cases of deliberate indifference but requires separate showing.
- Sidewalk maintenance per se (cracks, heaving, condition) is implicated to the extent it affects ADA accessibility but is governed by separate municipal-tort and city-ordinance frameworks.
what_this_is_not_simple:
A few things this case does NOT cover:
- SEPTA buses, trains, stations, or rail platforms. SEPTA has its own ADA obligations and complaints go to SEPTA directly.
- Sidewalks in townships outside Philadelphia city limits (Cheltenham, Lower Merion, etc.).
- Private property sidewalks — shopping centers, apartment complexes, etc. Those have separate Title III/private-property obligations.
- Crosswalk signal timing (some Title II claims about audible/tactile signals are separate).
- Other Philadelphia accessibility issues — recreation centers, libraries, schools. Each is separate.

If your barrier isn't about a city sidewalk curb ramp, Ada can help you find the right path.

questions.prompt: Do you use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or have another mobility disability?
What kind of provider was it? (Hospital, primary care, specialist, dentist, imaging center, urgent care, OB/GYN, mental health, other)
What's the provider name and location?
What was the problem? (Couldn't get on the exam table, no transfer help, inaccessible imaging machine, told to bring family, told to go elsewhere, etc.)
Did you get the care you needed, get inadequate care, get sent elsewhere, or give up?
Do you have written documentation of the refusal or any related correspondence?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Validate the experience — being denied care for disability reasons is dehumanizing and dangerous. Route to HHS OCR + DOJ + DRA + state medical board. Mention Fust v. First Urology as related precedent (and link via related_listing_ids if technically possible — see also dra-v-first-urology-medical-equipment).

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: provider identification (entity and specific facility); visit date; specific accommodation request and response; staff identification (where available); written referral or denial documentation; medical records demonstrating qualifying disability and care need; records of alternative care obtained or attempted; consequential health-impact documentation.
documentation_required_simple:
- Provider name and location.
- Date of visit or attempted visit.
- What was needed (transfer help, accessible table, accessible imaging machine, etc.).
- What happened.
- Names of staff if you have them.
- Any written denial or referral.
- Medical records showing impact on your care.
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a mobility disability — wheelchair, scooter, walker, paralysis, amputation, severe arthritis, etc. (Other disabilities also covered for related issues like communication access.)
- You attempted to receive medical care at any provider — hospital, primary care, specialist, urgent care, dental, OB/GYN, imaging center, mental health, etc.
- You couldn't access the equipment, weren't offered transfer assistance, were examined inadequately because of these failures, were told to go elsewhere, or were told to bring family for transfer.

This applies to public AND private medical providers, including those that take Medicare/Medicaid (which is essentially all of them).
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 seeking medical, dental, mental health, or related services at any healthcare provider subject to Title II, Title III, § 504, or § 1557 jurisdiction. The breadth of § 504 coverage through Medicare/Medicaid participation creates near-universal federal anti-discrimination jurisdiction over US healthcare providers.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You use a wheelchair, scooter, walker, or have another mobility disability.
- You went to a doctor, hospital, or clinic.
- They couldn't fit you with the right equipment, wouldn't help you transfer, or told you to go somewhere else.
evidence_guidance_professional: Pre-visit: written accommodation request documenting transfer needs and MDE requirements; preservation of provider response. At-visit: contemporaneous documentation of accommodation request, response, staff identification, and the actual care delivered (or not). Photographic evidence of inaccessible equipment where lawful. Post-visit: medical-records preservation; preservation of any alternative-care receipts; coordinate with HHS OCR + DOJ + DRA for parallel reporting.
evidence_guidance_simple:
Before the visit:
- Call ahead and ask: "Do you have a height-adjustable exam table? Will staff help me transfer?"
- Write down the answer and who said it.

At the visit:
- If you're refused, ask for documentation of the refusal.
- Get the staff name and the practice manager's name.
- Take photos of inaccessible equipment if you can.

After:
- Email yourself a summary while it's fresh.
- File the complaint quickly.
- Get all your medical records related to the visit.
full_description:
Medical providers — hospitals, primary care, specialists, imaging centers, urgent care, dental, mental health, OB/GYN, and others — frequently fail to provide accessible medical equipment or transfer assistance for patients with mobility disabilities. This results in patients being examined while still in their wheelchairs (medically inadequate), being denied imaging or procedures entirely, or being told to bring family members to help transfer.

The legal framework is robust:
- ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) covers most private medical providers.
- Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) covers public hospitals and clinics.
- Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794) covers all federally funded providers — which is nearly every healthcare provider through Medicare/Medicaid.
- Section 1557 of the ACA (42 U.S.C. § 18116) provides additional healthcare-specific anti-discrimination obligations.
- DOJ's May 2024 Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule (28 C.F.R. § 36.302(f)) requires accessible MDE — exam tables, weight scales, mammography, X-ray, MRI/CT positioning equipment — with phased acquisition and use obligations.

The MDE Rule sets specific standards based on the US Access Board MDE Standards. New equipment acquired after the effective date must meet the standards; existing equipment must be brought into compliance on a schedule.

Common violations:
- Fixed-height exam tables (a patient in a wheelchair can't get on it).
- No staff trained on transfer assistance.
- "Bring a family member" policies (this is what got First Urology in trouble — see related listing).
- Inaccessible imaging equipment.
- Denial of service or referral elsewhere for accommodation that should be provided.
- Communication failures with deaf patients (separate but related).

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights (Section 504 + Section 1557) at hhs.gov/ocr — primary enforcer for healthcare.
- State medical board for license/quality concerns.
- State Protection & Advocacy.
- Disability Rights Advocates (dralegal.org) — they litigated the First Urology case.
full_description_professional:
Medical equipment accessibility is governed by ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) for private providers; Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35) for public providers; Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794, 45 C.F.R. Part 84) for federally funded providers (nearly universal via Medicare/Medicaid); and Section 1557 of the ACA (42 U.S.C. § 18116, 45 C.F.R. Part 92).

DOJ's May 2024 final rule at 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(f) (Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule) implements the U.S. Access Board MDE Standards (36 C.F.R. Part 1195) with binding effect: covered entities must acquire MDE meeting the standards going forward and bring existing equipment into compliance on phased schedules. Equipment categories include exam tables, weight scales, mammography systems, radiography, MRI/CT positioning, and others.

Substantive obligations: (i) reasonable modifications to policies, practices, and procedures including transfer-assistance protocols; (ii) MDE acquisition meeting accessibility standards; (iii) staff training on transfer techniques and accommodation provision; (iv) effective-communication provision for deaf patients (28 C.F.R. § 36.303 + § 35.160); (v) no service denial or improper referral as a substitute for accommodation provision. The Fust v. First Urology pattern of class enforcement is now well-established.

Enforcement avenues: HHS OCR (primary healthcare enforcement; particularly responsive to § 1557 and § 504 claims); DOJ Civil Rights Division (Title III enforcement); state medical boards and licensing authorities; state P&A entities; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; private § 504 / § 1557 actions for individualized compensatory damages where deliberate indifference is established.
full_description_simple:
Doctors and hospitals have to make their exam tables and machines work for people with disabilities.

Examples of problems:
- The exam table is too high — you can't get on it from your wheelchair.
- Staff says "we don't help with transfers."
- The mammogram machine doesn't work for someone who can't stand.
- The MRI tube can't be entered from a wheelchair.
- The doctor examines you while you're still in your chair (which is bad medical care).
- They tell you to go somewhere else, like a hospital.
- They tell you to bring a family member to help.

The law:
- The ADA covers private doctors and hospitals.
- Section 504 covers any healthcare provider that takes Medicare or Medicaid (almost all of them).
- A new federal rule (May 2024) makes specific requirements about exam tables, weight scales, mammograms, and other equipment.

If you're dealing with this, the same thing happened to the patients in the Fust v. First Urology case (you can see that one in our case list).

Where to report:
- HHS Office for Civil Rights at hhs.gov/ocr — they handle healthcare complaints.
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Disability Rights Advocates at dralegal.org.
- Your state medical board.

key_dates.Related settled case: Fust v. First Urology (3:20-cv-00562 W.D. Ky.)

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: HHS OCR (primary healthcare enforcement); DOJ § 36.502; state medical/licensing boards (separate substantive standard, but creates pressure); state P&A; private § 504 / § 1557 counsel for individualized claims; DRA for systemic enforcement.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Where to report:
- HHS Office for Civil Rights at hhs.gov/ocr — best for healthcare.
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Disability Rights Advocates.
- Your state medical board (for licensing concerns).
- Your state Protection & Advocacy.
short_description: Medical providers without accessible exam tables, diagnostic equipment, or transfer policies. DOJ MDE rule (May 2024) makes new compliance deadlines enforceable.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III + Section 504 + Section 1557 framework for medical-equipment accessibility. DOJ's May 2024 Medical Diagnostic Equipment Rule (28 C.F.R. § 36.302(f)) establishes specific accessible-MDE acquisition and transfer-assistance obligations with phased compliance deadlines. Common targets: hospitals, primary care, specialist offices, imaging centers, urgent care, dental clinics.
short_description_simple: Your doctor's office, hospital, or clinic doesn't have an exam table or imaging machine you can actually use, OR staff refused to help you transfer from your wheelchair. This is illegal. A new federal rule (May 2024) makes the standards specific and enforceable.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: insurance-coverage disputes (separate ERISA + § 1557 + state-insurance framework); non-disability quality-of-care concerns (state medical-board licensing); pharmacy retail kiosks (see kiosk-accessibility); pharmacy medication-counseling (separate Title III + § 504 framework); hospital amenities/food service (separate Title III + facility-design framework); insurance-network limitations.
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered:
- Insurance denials (separate framework).
- Quality-of-care complaints unrelated to disability (state medical board).
- Pharmacy access (see pharmacy/kiosk-accessibility for self-service issues; pharmacy-specific medication-counseling accommodations are a separate Title III claim).
- Hospital food service or amenities (separate Title III claims, less robust enforcement).
- Provider-of-choice limits in your insurance plan.

questions.prompt: What is your disability and what assistive technology do you use? (VoiceOver, TalkBack, switch control, voice control, magnifier, etc.)
What app? (Company, app name)
What couldn't you do? (Read account balance, transfer money, complete checkout, contact support, etc.)
What state are you in? (NY, CA, MA, and others have stronger laws)
Can you describe specifically what your screen reader said (or didn't say) when the failure happened?
Have you contacted the company about the problem? What did they say?

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Validate the experience. This is an active enforcement area. Route to DOJ + state AG (emphasize NY/CA/MA stronger laws) + NFB/ACB. For users in stronger-law states, mention that monetary damages may be available.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: application identification (name, version, platform); device and operating system; assistive technology used (VoiceOver, TalkBack, switch control, voice control, magnifier); WCAG-failure-mode identification; specific user-task impact; screen-reader output capture where possible; reproduction of barrier across application updates if persistent.
documentation_required_simple:
- The app name and version.
- The company.
- Your phone and screen reader (iPhone VoiceOver, Android TalkBack, etc.).
- What you were trying to do.
- What didn't work.
- Screenshots or screen-reader output recordings if possible.
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You have a vision disability (blindness or low vision) — or other disability — that requires assistive technology to use mobile apps.
- You tried to use a mobile app for a financial service, retail, food, ride, or other consumer-facing service.
- The app didn't work with your assistive technology (VoiceOver, TalkBack, screen magnifier, switch control, voice control, etc.).

You don't have to have completed a transaction. Just attempting to use the app and encountering a barrier is enough.

Multi-jurisdictional consideration: if you're in New York, California, Massachusetts, or other state with strong parallel laws, your remedies may be significantly stronger including monetary damages.
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 using mobile applications for services covered by Title III's public-accommodation framework. Standing is established by access denial; transaction completion is not required. State-law parallel coverage (NY, CA, MA, etc.) may provide statutory damages unavailable under Title III alone.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- You use a screen reader (or other assistive tech) on your phone.
- You tried to use an app — bank, store, food, rideshare, etc.
- The app didn't work for you.

If you live in California, New York, or Massachusetts, the law there is even stronger and you might get money damages.
evidence_guidance_professional: Documentation: application and version identification; assistive-technology configuration; WCAG-failure-mode identification with success-criteria reference; screen-reader output capture (via concurrent screen recording on a secondary device, or with audio routing where supported); reproduction across application updates to demonstrate persistent inaccessibility; transactional-impact narrative.
evidence_guidance_simple:
- Record what your screen reader is saying when the app fails (you can use a separate device or the phone's screen recording).
- Take screenshots of the screens that don't work.
- Write a clear narrative: app, version, what you tried to do, what happened.
- File the complaint while it's fresh.
full_description:
Mobile apps — especially banking apps, retail apps, food delivery, rideshare, and government-service apps — frequently don't work with iPhone's VoiceOver or Android's TalkBack. This makes them unusable for blind and low-vision people.

This is an emerging area of disability-rights law. Courts have generally held that mobile apps are covered by ADA Title III when there's a "nexus" between the app and a physical place of public accommodation (a bank branch, a store, etc.). Coverage of pure-online services is more contested, but the trend is toward broader coverage.

Common mobile-app accessibility violations:
- Unlabeled buttons (VoiceOver says "Button" instead of "Transfer Money").
- Inaccessible drag-and-drop interactions.
- CAPTCHAs that aren't accessible.
- Image-only content with no alt text (account balances rendered as images of numbers).
- Custom controls that don't expose state to screen readers.
- Mandatory tutorials that block screen-reader users from completing setup.
- Authentication systems that don't work with assistive technology.

The legal framework:
- ADA Title III (28 C.F.R. Part 36) — public-accommodation coverage.
- Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794) — if the company receives federal funding.
- State laws: New York General Business Law § 349, California Unruh Act, Massachusetts Public Accommodation Act, and others provide stronger remedies including monetary damages.
- DOJ has not yet issued mobile-specific regulations but has signaled enforcement intent.

Notable litigation:
- NFB v. PNC Bank — pending.
- NFB v. Bank of America — settled with accessibility commitments.
- Numerous retail-app and food-service-app cases.

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- The company's accessibility office or customer service (creates notice).
- State attorney general consumer-protection division.
- For NY, CA, MA, and similar states, the state civil-rights agency.
- NFB (nfb.org) and ACB (acb.org) for systemic pattern documentation.
- Private disability-rights counsel.
full_description_professional:
Mobile-application accessibility is an emerging area of ADA Title III litigation, with Title III coverage of mobile apps generally established through the "nexus theory" linking digital products to underlying physical public accommodations (Robles v. Domino's, 913 F.3d 898 (9th Cir. 2019); cert. denied, 140 S. Ct. 122 (2019)). The 11th Circuit's Gil v. Winn-Dixie precedent went the other direction, creating circuit split, though many post-Robles decisions have followed Robles's reasoning.

Substantive WCAG 2.1/2.2 success criteria implicate: 1.1.1 (alt text), 1.3.1 (info and relationships), 1.4.3 (contrast), 2.1.1 (keyboard accessibility, including for switch users), 2.4.7 (focus visibility), 4.1.2 (ARIA name/role/value), and mobile-specific criteria around touch targets (2.5.5) and motion-activated alternatives (2.5.4).

Concurrent state-law frameworks provide stronger remedies: New York General Business Law § 349 (deceptive-practices framework, statutory damages); California Unruh Civil Rights Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§ 51 & 52 ($4,000 per offense statutory damages); Massachusetts Public Accommodation Act; New York City Human Rights Law (NYCHRL § 8-107).

Notable litigation: NFB v. PNC Bank (ongoing); NFB v. Bank of America (settled); financial-services and retail-app dockets are extensive.

Enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division (Title III, 28 C.F.R. § 36.502); state AG civil-rights divisions; state P&A; private Title III counsel for nexus-theory cases; state-law parallel actions for statutory damages where available; coordination with NFB, ACB, NDRN, DRA for systemic pattern documentation.
full_description_simple:
Apps on your phone — like banking apps, store apps, food delivery apps — often don't work for blind people.

The screen reader on your phone (VoiceOver on iPhone, TalkBack on Android) reads what's on the screen out loud. But when an app isn't designed right, the screen reader says "Button" instead of telling you what the button does. Or the app shows your bank balance as a picture instead of text. Or the buttons need you to drag instead of tap.

This area of law is still being figured out by courts. But many cases have already been settled or are in progress:
- Bank of America settled.
- PNC Bank case is pending.
- Many retail and food delivery apps have been sued.

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The company's customer service or accessibility office.
- Your state attorney general.
- The National Federation of the Blind (nfb.org).

full_description:
This was a federal class-action lawsuit against Uber Technologies, Inc. and its UberX subsidiary by the National Federation of the Blind (NFB), its California affiliate, and three individual blind riders — Michael Hingson, Michael Kelly, and Michael Pedersen. They sued in September 2014 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. The case is settled and now in compliance phase.

Plaintiffs alleged that UberX drivers were routinely refusing to transport blind riders accompanied by guide dogs or other service animals, and that Uber wasn't taking adequate steps to stop the discrimination. Drivers cited allergies, fear of dogs, religious objections, or just refused without explanation. Uber was charged separately for "cleaning fees" related to service animals in some cases. The plaintiffs argued this violated ADA Title III's prohibition on disability discrimination by transportation services, as well as California's Unruh Act and Disabled Persons Act.

The case settled in December 2016 with comprehensive injunctive relief:
- Uber agreed that drivers must transport service-animal users.
- Knowing refusals get the driver permanently removed from the platform. Repeated plausible complaints (even without intent) also result in permanent removal.
- Allergy or fear of dogs is not a valid excuse — those drivers also must transport.
- Uber must track every complaint and report aggregated data to class counsel.
- NFB and its California affiliate run a testing program with blind riders.
- A third-party monitor (retired Judge Margaret A. Nagle) oversees compliance.
- Cleaning-fee policies for service-animal-related issues were reformed.

The settlement ran for an initial 3.5 years, with provisions for a 1.5-year extension if Uber wasn't in substantial compliance. As of July 2020 the court's jurisdiction was still active. The case awarded $2.38M in attorneys' fees; the three named plaintiffs received $15,000 each; NFB receives ongoing annual payments.

If an Uber driver refuses to take you because of your service animal today, you should still report it. The settlement made enforcement easier, but driver-level discrimination still happens. Plaintiffs are represented by Disability Rights Advocates, TRE Legal, and Rosen Bien Galvan & Grunfeld.
full_description_professional:
National Federation of the Blind of California, et al. v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 3:14-cv-04086 (N.D. Cal., filed Sept. 9, 2014), was a class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq.; the California Unruh Civil Rights Act, Cal. Civ. Code §§ 51 & 52; and the California Disabled Persons Act, Cal. Civ. Code § 54. Plaintiffs NFB, NFB-CA, and three individual blind riders (Michael Hingson, Michael Kelly, Michael Pedersen) alleged Uber's on-demand transportation network discriminated against blind riders with service animals through driver-level refusals, allergic/phobic exclusion, and discriminatory cleaning-fee assessments.

The DOJ filed a Statement of Interest on Dec. 23, 2014 supporting plaintiffs, citing DOT regulations at 49 C.F.R. § 37.3 and pt. 37, app. D § 37.23 (Title III applies to "demand-responsive" services without fixed routes; private entities cannot contract away ADA obligations; entities must permit service animals to accompany owners into vehicles).

The Dec. 6, 2016 settlement (judgment entered Dec. 15, 2016) provides comprehensive injunctive relief: (i) driver-platform acknowledgments via pop-up interactive questionnaire and contract amendments; (ii) quarterly email reminders to drivers; (iii) permanent platform removal for knowing service-animal refusals; (iv) permanent platform removal for repeated plausible complaints regardless of intent; (v) prohibition of allergy or fear-of-dogs defenses; (vi) cleaning-fee policy reform; (vii) complaint-tracking and aggregated reporting to class counsel; (viii) NFB-administered testing program with blind riders; (ix) retired Judge Margaret A. Nagle appointed as neutral monitor with authority to extend the agreement by 1.5 years for substantial non-compliance.

Monetary terms: $2.38M attorneys' fees and costs; $15,000 individual damages each named plaintiff; $75,000/yr to NFB during settlement term, with provision for fourth-year payment if extended. As of July 2020 the court retained ongoing jurisdiction; the settlement enforcement mechanism remains active.

Plaintiffs represented by Disability Rights Advocates (DRA), TRE Legal Practice, and Rosen Bien Galvan & Grunfeld LLP.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against Uber. It is now settled. The settlement is still in effect — it tells Uber what to do.

Three blind men who use guide dogs sued Uber in 2014. They said Uber drivers kept refusing to give them rides because of their dogs. Drivers said things like "I'm allergic" or "I don't like dogs." Sometimes they drove away when they saw the dog. This is against the ADA — businesses have to serve people with service animals, even if the driver doesn't like dogs.

In 2016 the case was settled. Now Uber has to:
- Tell every driver: you must give rides to people with service animals.
- Permanently remove drivers who knowingly refuse.
- Permanently remove drivers who get more than one complaint, even if they say they didn't mean it.
- Tell drivers that allergies and fear of dogs are NOT a good reason.
- Track every complaint and tell the lawyers about them.
- Let the National Federation of the Blind send blind test riders to check on drivers.
- Reform their cleaning fee rules so people don't get charged extra because of a service animal.

If a driver refuses you today, you can:
- Report it to Uber inside the app.
- Tell the National Federation of the Blind testing program.
- Call Disability Rights Advocates.
- File a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase; ongoing monitoring

no_documentation_path_professional:

Reporting channels:

short_description: Settled Dec 2016. Uber required to enforce its service-animal anti-discrimination policy nationwide, track complaints, and remove drivers who refuse service to blind riders with guide dogs. Court retains ongoing jurisdiction. Report current discrimination incidents.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under Title III ADA, California Unruh Civil Rights Act, and California Disabled Persons Act. Dec 2016 settlement (Dkt. 137, J.) requires nationwide policy enforcement: driver pop-up acknowledgment of service-animal obligation, complaint-tracking with aggregated reporting to class counsel, permanent platform removal for knowing or repeated refusal, NFB/DRA testing program, retired-Judge Margaret A. If an Uber driver refuses to take you because of your service animal, you can report it — and they take it seriously now because of this lawsuit.
what_this_is_not_professional:
Scope limitations:
- Binds Uber Technologies, Inc. and affiliates only. Lyft (subject to separate litigation, including the recently consolidated multi-district matter), traditional taxis, and microtransit operators require separate enforcement.
- Limited to the rideshare transportation context. Uber's non-rider businesses (Uber Eats, Uber Freight, Jump bicycles, etc.) are not within settlement scope.
- Covers trained service animals as defined under 28 C.F.R. § 36.104. Emotional support animals, untrained companion animals, and non-canine animals (with limited exceptions for miniature horses under DOT regulations) are not within settlement coverage.
- Title III generally does not provide compensatory damages; available remedies under federal law are injunctive plus attorneys' fees. Statutory damages may be available under the California Unruh Act ($4,000 per offense) and analogous state statutes (NYCHRL, MA PAA).
- The settlement focuses on driver-level discrimination. Uber's overall service-design accessibility (app screen-reader compatibility, driver-rider communication, etc.) may have separate Title III implications not pleaded here.

For barriers outside scope:
- Lyft: separate DRA-led litigation paths and DOJ complaints.
- Other rideshare/microtransit: DOJ administrative complaints under § 36.502.
- Non-rideshare service-animal denials: separate Title III actions in the relevant public-accommodation context.
what_this_is_not_simple:
A few things this case does NOT cover:
- Lyft. Lyft has its own separate lawsuits and rules.
- Taxis, traditional cabs, or local livery services. They're covered by the ADA but not by this settlement.
- Uber Eats, Uber Freight, Uber for Business — different services with different rules.
- Service-animal access at restaurants, stores, hotels, etc. Those are covered by Title III but separately.
- Untrained pets or "emotional support animals." The settlement is specifically about trained service animals under the ADA definition.
- Damages for past discrimination if you're not in California. The Unruh Act provides money damages for Californians; federal ADA Title III generally doesn't for any state.

If your issue is with a different rideshare service, a taxi, or a different setting — Ada can help find the right path.

questions.prompt: Do you use a wheelchair as your main way to get around?
Is your wheelchair use because of a long-term disability, not a temporary injury?

Have you tried to book a stay, or actually stayed, at a Hilton-brand hotel since 2022? (Includes Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, Conrad, or Hilton Grand Vacations.)

questions.prompt: Was the bed in the accessible room too high or too low for you to safely move into from your wheelchair?

questions.purpose: Drives documentation_required vs no_documentation_path routing in conversation.

ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Ask questions one at a time. Confirm understanding before moving to the next. If the user says no to question 1 or 2, gently explain this specific case might not be a fit and offer to discuss other accessibility cases. If they say yes to questions 1-3, lean into the bed-height story. If yes on 4 or 5, validate the experience and explain what happens next. If no on 6, route to the no-documentation off-ramp and offer the DOJ-complaint path.

documentation_required_professional:
The following documentation, while not strictly required for class membership, will substantially strengthen a class member's position:
- Reservation records, booking confirmations, or stay receipts from any Hilton-branded property identifying dates and room designations.
- Photographic evidence of the inaccessible bed surface, ideally with scale reference (e.g., wheelchair adjacent to bed, tape measure, or comparison object).
- Written communications with Hilton reservation channels evidencing inquiries about bed-height or transfer-surface accessibility and Hilton's responses (or non-responses).
- Medical or rehabilitation documentation establishing the qualifying mobility disability and wheelchair dependence (typically a treating physician's note, prosthetist or DME provider records, or VA disability rating documentation).
- A narrative description of the access barrier encountered or the basis for deterred booking.

Class members are not required to litigate or prove the substantive Title III violation; the named plaintiff and class counsel carry that burden. Documentation supports the class-membership inquiry and any individualized relief that may eventually be available.
documentation_required_simple:
What helps your case if you have it:
- The dates and locations of Hilton-brand hotels you stayed at or tried to book.
- Reservation confirmations or emails from Hilton.
- Photos of the bed in an "accessible" room — especially photos that show the height (a tape measure or wheelchair next to it is great).
- Any messages where you asked Hilton about the bed height and they couldn't or wouldn't tell you.
- Notes from a doctor or therapist about the wheelchair you use and why.

You don't need all of these. Even one or two of these things can be enough to be part of the class. The lawsuit isn't asking you to prove the legal case yourself — the lawyers do that — they just need to confirm you fit the class definition.
eligibility:
This case is for travelers who rely on a wheelchair for mobility and who have looked into staying — or actually stayed — at a Hilton-brand hotel since 2022. The putative class as defined in the complaint covers anyone with a qualifying mobility disability who uses a standard-sized wheelchair and who has accessed Hilton's website or called a Hilton hotel seeking information about the accessibility of its rooms.

You may be part of the class if all of the following apply:
- You use a wheelchair (manual or power) as your primary means of mobility.
- You have a qualifying mobility-related disability.
- You looked at booking, attempted to book, or actually stayed in a Hilton-brand hotel — including Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, Conrad, or Hilton Grand Vacations — during a period the lawsuit is expected to cover (approximately 2022 to the present).
- You either could not safely transfer to the bed in a designated "accessible" room, or you avoided booking because Hilton wouldn't tell you the bed height in advance.

You don't need to have been physically injured to be part of this case. The harm the lawsuit recognizes includes being denied equal access, not just physical injury.
eligibility_professional:
The putative class, as pleaded, comprises all persons in the United States who: (1) possess a qualifying mobility disability within the meaning of 42 U.S.C. § 12102; (2) rely on a standard-sized wheelchair as a primary means of locomotion; and (3) during the class period have accessed Hilton's online reservation system or telephonic reservation channel seeking information regarding the accessibility of designated mobility-accessible rooms at any Hilton-branded property (including, without limitation, properties operating under the Hilton, Conrad, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hampton Inn, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, and Hilton Grand Vacations brands).

Class certification under Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(a) and (b)(2) is sought. Standing is grounded in the dignitary and equal-access injuries recognized by the ADA framework; physical injury is not a prerequisite. Class membership does not require a completed stay — a wheelchair user who was deterred from booking because Hilton failed to provide accessibility information sufficient to make an informed reservation may also fall within the class definition.
eligibility_simple:
This case is for you if all of these are true:
- You use a wheelchair to get around.
- You have a disability that affects your mobility.
- You tried to book a stay at a Hilton-brand hotel — Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Waldorf Astoria, or others.
- You either:
  - couldn't safely get into the bed in the "accessible" room, OR
  - didn't book because Hilton wouldn't tell you how high the bed was.

You don't need to have fallen or been hurt to be part of this case. The harm is being shut out, even if nothing bad physically happened to you.
evidence_guidance_professional:
For documentation of an active stay or a recent encounter:

At reservation:
- Capture screenshots of the Hilton reservation interface depicting the accessibility-information surfaces (or their absence). Note any specific bed-height representations made.
- If reserving by telephone, log the date, time, agent name (or call-center identifier), and the substance of any accessibility inquiry and response.

At check-in and during stay:
- Photograph the bed surface from a perspective that establishes vertical height. Recommended: side-elevation view with a measuring tape extended from floor to mattress top, or wheelchair positioned adjacent with seat-height reference visible.
- Capture room-designation signage and reservation confirmation showing the room was designated mobility-accessible.
- Document the entire room: bathroom grab bars and clearance, path of travel, doorway widths, transfer-surface heights at toilet and shower/tub.
- Note the property's brand and street address; many Hilton brands are managed by third-party operators, and brand identification matters for the class definition.

When raising the issue with hotel staff:
- Log staff names and titles, date and time, the specific accommodation requested, and the response.
- If alternate accommodations (different room, alternative property, supplemental equipment) are offered, document the offer; if not, document the absence.

Preserve all such records in a single dated folder or cloud archive. Class counsel typically request such records when opting in or for individualized relief proceedings.
evidence_guidance_simple:
If you're documenting a stay at a Hilton hotel right now, or planning one, here's what helps most:

When you reserve:
- Use the Hilton website or call. Ask specifically about bed height in their mobility-accessible rooms. Save the answer (screenshot the chat, take notes from the call with the date and the agent's name if you can).

When you arrive:
- Take photos of the bed BEFORE you change anything. Show the floor-to-mattress height. A tape measure helps a lot. So does putting your wheelchair next to it for scale.
- Take photos of the rest of the room too — bathroom grab bars, the entry, the path between the bed and bathroom.
- Write down the room number, the date, and what brand the hotel is (Hilton, Hampton Inn, etc.).

If you try to ask for help:
- Take notes on who you spoke to at the front desk and what they offered (or didn't).
- If they tell you they can't lower the bed or move you to a different room, write that down too.

Save everything in one place — emails, photos, notes. If your phone has a "favorites" folder for photos, use it.
full_description:
This is a federal class-action lawsuit. Christopher Niles, a wheelchair user, sued Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Hilton Management LLC, and Hilton Management Services in February 2026 in Pennsylvania federal court. The case is being heard in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania.

The lawsuit alleges that when Hilton designates a room as "accessible" or "mobility-accessible," the bed in that room is often too high or too low for a wheelchair user to safely and independently transfer to. A standard wheelchair seat sits 18-20 inches from the floor; a typical hotel mattress surface sits 25-30 inches or higher. That gap can make an otherwise accessible room unusable. The complaint also alleges that Hilton's website and call-center don't tell guests the bed height in advance, so wheelchair users can't tell whether a so-called "accessible" room will actually work for them until they've already arrived and checked in.

The lawsuit covers Hilton's full brand family — Hilton, Conrad, DoubleTree, Embassy Suites, Hampton Inn, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, Home2 Suites, Waldorf Astoria, Hilton Grand Vacations, and others operated under the Hilton corporate umbrella. The plaintiff is seeking declaratory and injunctive relief — meaning, an order from the court requiring Hilton to fix the problem — plus class certification on behalf of similarly affected travelers. No monetary damages have been sought for individual class members at this stage.

The case is in its early phases. It has been filed but has not yet been certified as a class action, and Hilton has not yet been required to answer the complaint formally.
full_description_professional:
Niles v. Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc., et al., 2:26-cv-00258 (W.D. Pa., filed Feb. 12, 2026), is a putative class action under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and its implementing regulations, 28 C.F.R. Part 36.

The complaint alleges that Hilton's mobility-accessible guest rooms across its brand portfolio contain bed surfaces whose vertical height renders safe, independent wheelchair-to-bed transfer impracticable for guests dependent on standard manual or power wheelchairs (seat heights of approximately 18-20 inches). Plaintiff alleges this constitutes a failure to provide an "equal opportunity" to use the lodging services provided to nondisabled guests, in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 12182(a) and the ADA Standards for Accessible Design as incorporated into 28 C.F.R. § 36.406.

The complaint further alleges that Hilton's online reservation systems and telephonic reservation channels fail to provide accurate accessibility information regarding bed dimensions sufficient to enable wheelchair users to assess whether a designated accessible room will, in fact, be accessible to them — implicating Hilton's obligations under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(e) (reservations) and the 2010 DOJ Consent Decree, United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc., 1:10-cv-01902 (D.D.C.).

Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief, certification of a nationwide class of persons with qualifying mobility disabilities who rely on standard wheelchairs and have accessed Hilton's reservation channels seeking accessibility information, attorneys' fees and costs under 42 U.S.C. § 12205, and a jury trial. The matter has been assigned to the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania.
full_description_simple:
This is a class-action lawsuit against Hilton hotels. The lawsuit was filed in February 2026 in a federal court in Pennsylvania.

A man who uses a wheelchair stayed in Hilton rooms that the hotel said were "accessible." The beds in those rooms were too high. He could not safely get from his wheelchair into the bed. A standard wheelchair seat is about 18 to 20 inches off the floor. Most hotel mattresses are 25 to 30 inches off the floor — or even higher. That makes the bed too high to use.

The lawsuit also says Hilton's website and phone reservations don't tell people how high the beds are. So a wheelchair user can't know in advance if the bed will work for them.

The lawsuit covers all Hilton brands — including Hilton, Hampton Inn, Embassy Suites, DoubleTree, Hilton Garden Inn, Homewood Suites, and Waldorf Astoria. The plaintiff is asking the court to make Hilton fix this. He is not asking for money for individual class members yet.

The case has been filed. It has not yet been certified as a class action by the judge.

key_dates.Status: Early pleadings

no_documentation_path_professional:
Absence of documentary evidence does not foreclose class membership. The class definition pleaded by Plaintiff is intentionally broad: it encompasses individuals who accessed Hilton's reservation channels seeking accessibility information, whether or not a reservation was ultimately consummated and whether or not the prospective guest preserved contemporaneous records.

Class members who lack contemporaneous documentation should nonetheless consider the following parallel avenues:
- Filing an administrative complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice, Civil Rights Division, Disability Rights Section, under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502. The DOJ retains independent enforcement authority over Title III public accommodations and has a documented enforcement history with Hilton (see United States v. Hilton Worldwide Inc., 1:10-cv-01902 (D.D.C. 2010) consent decree).
- Submitting a direct demand letter to Hilton's general counsel describing the access barrier encountered. While such a letter is not a substitute for litigation participation, it may preserve a colorable individual claim and, in some jurisdictions, satisfy pre-suit notice obligations under analogous state public-accommodations statutes.
- Documenting the experience contemporaneously going forward: dates, locations, room designations, and any communications with Hilton reservation channels.

These avenues do not require contemporaneous documentation of past stays.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You may still be part of this case even if you don't have receipts, photos, or messages saved.

If you remember roughly when and where you tried to book a Hilton room or stayed at one, and you couldn't safely use the bed because of your wheelchair, that's still a real experience the lawyers want to hear about. They can sometimes pull records from Hilton's own systems through the legal process.

If you don't have documentation, you might also consider:
- Filing a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice about Hilton specifically. The DOJ has gone after Hilton before on accessibility issues.
- Writing to Hilton directly about what happened. Even if nothing comes of it, it creates a record.

Talk to Ada about your situation. She can help you figure out what makes sense for you.
short_description: Hilton designates rooms as mobility-accessible without ensuring beds permit safe wheelchair transfer.
short_description_professional: Class action alleges Hilton's designated mobility-accessible rooms contain bed surfaces that prevent safe, independent wheelchair-to-bed transfer, and Hilton fails to disclose bed-height information at the time of reservation.
short_description_simple: Hilton's accessible hotel rooms often have beds that are too high to safely move into from a wheelchair.
what_this_is_not_professional:
Scope limitations of this action:
- The defendants are limited to Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc., Hilton Management LLC, and Hilton Management Services Inc. and the Hilton-branded properties subject to their control. Properties operated under non-Hilton flags (Marriott, Hyatt, IHG, Choice, Wyndham, Best Western, independent hotels) are outside the class definition.
- The alleged violation is narrowly bounded to bed-surface accessibility and the related reservation-disclosure failure. Other Title III violations at Hilton properties — restroom non-compliance under 28 C.F.R. § 36.406 / ADA Standards § 9.1, communication-access failures under 28 C.F.R. § 36.303, service-animal denials under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c), transportation-services denials, parking and path-of-travel barriers — are not pleaded in this complaint and would require separate enforcement.
- The relief sought is declaratory and injunctive; individualized compensatory damages are not pleaded under Title III, which does not provide for them. Title III does provide for attorneys' fees and costs.
- The complaint addresses guest-room bed surfaces in commercial lodging. It does not extend to medical, residential, or institutional sleeping surfaces governed by different regulatory frameworks.

For barriers outside this case's scope, parallel enforcement avenues include direct DOJ complaints, state public-accommodations claims, and analogous private actions under state civil-rights statutes such as California's Unruh Civil Rights Act, New York Human Rights Law, or the Pennsylvania Human Relations Act.
what_this_is_not_simple:
A few things this case does NOT cover:
- Hotels that are not Hilton-brand. Marriott, Hyatt, Best Western, Choice Hotels, and independent hotels are not part of this lawsuit.
- Other accessibility problems at Hilton that don't involve the bed — like a missing grab bar, a narrow doorway, or a broken elevator. Those might still be valid ADA issues but aren't part of THIS lawsuit.
- Service-animal denials, shuttle access, pool lifts, parking — also separate.
- Money damages for past stays. The lawsuit is asking the court to make Hilton fix the problem going forward, not to pay each class member.
- Hospital beds or medical-equipment beds in any setting. The case is about regular hotel beds in regular guest rooms.

If your situation involves something other than bed height at a Hilton hotel, Ada can help you find the right path — there are other cases and complaint channels.

You can report if:

On Election Day:

full_description:
Voting access for people with disabilities is one of the most active areas of federal civil-rights enforcement. Federal law requires:
- Physical access to polling places (ADA Title II + ADA Standards, 28 C.F.R. § 35.151).
- At least one accessible voting machine per polling place (Help America Vote Act — HAVA, 52 U.S.C. § 21081).
- Curbside voting for voters who can't safely enter (state-law requirement in most states, plus Title II program-access mandate).
- Accessible mail-in or absentee ballots where mail voting is provided to non-disabled voters (Title II equal-access standard).
- Poll worker training on disability access.

Despite these requirements, voting access barriers remain widespread:
- Polling places at inaccessible churches, schools, or community centers.
- Accessible voting machines that aren't deployed, aren't set up, or aren't functional.
- Poll workers unfamiliar with the accessible equipment.
- Curbside voting refused or unstaffed.
- Mail-in ballots delivered only on paper, requiring sighted assistance for blind voters.
- Audio-ballot equipment broken or set up in conspicuous locations destroying ballot privacy.

For specific jurisdictions with active matters, see:
- Bryant v. Harris County (TX) — current litigation over mail-ballot inaccessibility.
- DOJ v. LA County — current settlement over vote-center accessibility.
- DOJ investigation of Alaska Division of Elections — ongoing.

For voting problems in OTHER jurisdictions, this listing covers your reporting options.

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section at ADA.gov.
- DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section directly: voting.section@usdoj.gov.
- Your state Secretary of State (HAVA enforcement).
- Your state Protection & Advocacy.
- Your state attorney general.
- Disability rights legal organizations and the Election Protection coalition (866-OUR-VOTE).
full_description_professional:
Voter access for persons with disabilities is governed by overlapping federal frameworks: ADA Title II (28 C.F.R. Part 35, including § 35.151 ADA Standards for polling places); the Help America Vote Act, 52 U.S.C. § 21081 (accessible voting equipment requirements; one accessible voting system per polling place); Section 504 (29 U.S.C. § 794) for federally funded electoral activity; the Voting Rights Act, 52 U.S.C. §§ 10301 et seq. (general disability-related provisions); and the National Voter Registration Act (motor-voter accessibility).

Substantive obligations span:
- Polling-place physical access under 28 C.F.R. § 35.151 and the 2010 ADA Standards.
- HAVA-mandated accessible voting equipment deployment (52 U.S.C. § 21081), with one accessible system per polling place.
- Effective communication for deaf voters at polling places (28 C.F.R. § 35.160).
- Mail-ballot accessibility under Title II's equal-access standard (the Bryant v. Harris County theory).
- Curbside voting under state-law mandates and Title II program-access.

For specific active jurisdiction matters: Bryant v. Harris County (S.D. Tex.) — mail ballot RAVBM; DOJ v. LA County (settled August 2024) — vote-center accessibility; DOJ investigation of Alaska Division of Elections (ongoing). This listing covers other jurisdictions.

Enforcement: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (highly active enforcement area); state Secretary of State (HAVA administration); state P&A (election-protection capacity); Election Protection coalition (1-866-OUR-VOTE, real-time election-day support); private Title II actions; § 1983 actions for individual constitutional voting-rights violations.
full_description_simple:
Voting needs to be accessible to people with disabilities. Federal law has very strong protections.

Federal law says:
- Polling places must be wheelchair accessible.
- Every polling place must have at least one accessible voting machine.
- Curbside voting must be offered if you can't go inside.
- If your state lets people vote by mail, those ballots must be accessible to print-disabled voters too.

But problems are still common:
- The polling place is up steps with no ramp.
- The accessible voting machine isn't plugged in, or no one knows how to set it up.
- Curbside voting isn't offered or staff doesn't come outside.
- Paper mail ballots can't be read by blind voters with screen readers.

If your specific state/county has an active case, check our case list (Bryant v. Harris County for Texas, DOJ v. LA County for LA, the Alaska investigation).

If your problem is somewhere else:
- File a complaint with DOJ at ADA.gov.
- Call DOJ Voting Section: voting.section@usdoj.gov.
- Tell your state Secretary of State.
- Tell Election Protection: 866-OUR-VOTE (866-687-8683).
- Tell your state Protection & Advocacy office.

key_dates.Status: Active continuous enforcement

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division Voting Section (substantively responsive on voting-access claims); state Secretary of State (HAVA administrative complaint); state P&A (voting-access advocacy capacity); Election Protection (1-866-OUR-VOTE for real-time and post-election support); state AG civil-rights; private Title II / § 1983 counsel.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without paperwork:

- Election Protection: 1-866-OUR-VOTE (especially good for real-time problems on Election Day).
- Your state Secretary of State (HAVA enforcement).

short_description_professional: ADA Title II + HAVA + Section 504 framework for polling-place and ballot accessibility. Common violations: physical access barriers, accessible-voting-machine deployment/operability failures, curbside-voting refusals, inaccessible mail-in ballots for print-disabled voters. For jurisdiction-specific active cases see Bryant v. Harris County (TX mail-ballot RAVBM) and DOJ v. LA County (vote-center accessibility); this listing covers other jurisdictions.

what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: voter-ID requirements (VRA + § 2 framework); voter-list maintenance under NVRA; polling-place closures (VRA + § 5 considerations for covered jurisdictions); election-integrity non-disability claims; specific active jurisdictions with their own listed cases (Bryant v. Harris County, DOJ v. LA County, Alaska investigation).
