ada_qualifying_questions.voice_guidance: Validate the experience — service-animal refusals are dehumanizing and surprisingly common. Distinguish service animals from ESAs gently if needed (some users may have been led to believe ESAs are service animals). Route to DOJ + state AG + handler organizations. For statutory-damages states, mention monetary remedies. For repeat-violator businesses, suggest private Title III counsel.

documentation_required_professional: Documentation: business identification (chain and specific location); date/time; staff identification; verbatim statements (particularly impermissible inquiries — demands for documentation, vest requirements, task demonstrations — and impermissible defenses — "no pets," "allergies," etc.); witness identification; proof of attempted patronage (receipt, dated photo, etc.); description of service animal's trained tasks (not required for class membership but useful for establishing handler relationship).
documentation_required_simple:
- The business name and location.
- Date and approximate time.
- Names of staff involved.
- What they said — exact words matter (especially anything about "no dogs" or "we don't allow pets" or "you need paperwork").
- A witness, if anyone was with you.
- Receipt or proof you were there (or tried to be).
eligibility:
You can report a problem if:
- You use a trained service animal (specifically, a dog trained to perform work or tasks because of your disability).
- A restaurant, store, hotel, hospital, mall, theater, gym, taxi, or other public-accommodation business refused you entry, kicked you out, demanded documentation, required a vest, charged you extra, or otherwise treated you differently because of your service animal.

NOT eligible under this framework:
- Emotional support animals (ESAs) — covered under FHA for housing, not under ADA for restaurants/stores.
- Pets and untrained companion animals.
- For Uber/Lyft specifically, see rideshare-wheelchair-service-animal-denials.
- For airline travel with service animals, see airline-wheelchair-damage (related ACAA framework).
eligibility_professional: Coverage extends to handlers of dogs (and miniature horses under § 36.302(c)(9)) trained to perform work or tasks for persons with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102. Coverage does NOT extend to emotional support animals (covered under FHA and ACAA, not ADA Title III) or untrained companion animals.
eligibility_simple:
You can report if:
- Your dog is a trained service animal (not an emotional support animal — those have different rules).
- A restaurant, store, hotel, or other business wouldn't let you in (or kicked you out) because of your dog.
evidence_guidance_professional:
On-site: contemporaneous documentation of impermissible inquiries (documentation/vest/task-demonstration demands) and impermissible defenses ("no pets" / allergies / fear). Manager engagement to escalate refusal up the chain. Photographic capture of business and entrance. Witness identification.

Post-incident: narrative preservation within 24 hours; parallel administrative complaints (DOJ + state); coordinate with handler organizations for pattern documentation; for repeat violators or systemic patterns, retain private Title III counsel; for statutory-damages states (CA, NY, MA), state-law claims are typically the strongest leverage.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When it happens:
- Stay calm. Ask the staff member: "Are you refusing me because of my service dog?"
- Listen for and remember what they say back. Write it down as soon as you can.
- Ask for the manager and repeat the question.
- Get the business name, the manager's name, and date/time.
- Photograph the entrance or interior if you can do so safely.

After:
- Email yourself a complete narrative within 24 hours.
- File the complaint with DOJ and state agencies.
- Tell handler organizations — patterns help systemic enforcement.
full_description:
Service-animal access is one of the most clearly established areas of ADA law. Under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c), public accommodations (restaurants, stores, hotels, hospitals, malls, theaters, gyms, taxis, and many others) must permit service animals to accompany their handlers in all areas open to the general public.

The law is clear:
- Staff may ask only TWO questions: (1) Is the dog a service animal required because of a disability? (2) What work or task has it been trained to perform?
- Staff may NOT ask for documentation, a vest, an ID, or a demonstration.
- Allergies and fear of dogs are NOT valid reasons to deny access. Both individuals can be accommodated (separating the service animal and the allergic/fearful person to different areas of the room).
- "No pets" policies do not apply to service animals.
- Cleaning fees specifically for service animals are illegal.
- Service animals must be permitted in food preparation areas (this surprises restaurant owners but is settled law).

NOT covered:
- Emotional support animals (ESAs) — covered under Fair Housing Act for housing, but NOT under ADA for public accommodations.
- Pets and untrained companion animals.
- Animals other than dogs (miniature horses have limited additional coverage under DOT-specific rules).

Where to report:
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- The business's corporate accessibility office.
- Your state attorney general.
- For California, file with CRD for Unruh Act statutory damages ($4,000 per offense).
- For New York City, file with NYCCHR.
- Disability rights legal organizations (NFB, Guide Dogs for the Blind, NAGDU, etc. for advocacy).
- For rideshare/taxi specifically, see rideshare-wheelchair-service-animal-denials.
full_description_professional:
Service-animal access is governed by ADA Title III, 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c), and parallel Title II provisions. The framework establishes a near-absolute right of access for service-animal handlers to all areas open to the public at Title III public accommodations, subject only to legitimate safety concerns articulated under § 36.208 (safety threats based on individualized assessment) and § 36.302(c)(2) (housebreaking/under-control requirements).

Permissible inquiries are strictly limited to 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c)(6): (1) whether the dog is required because of a disability; (2) what work or task the dog has been trained to perform. Documentation, identification, and task-demonstration requirements are impermissible. Allergies, fear, religious objection, and "no pets" policies are not cognizable defenses. Surcharges or cleaning fees specifically targeting service animals are barred by § 36.301(c).

Service animals as defined under 28 C.F.R. § 36.104 are limited to dogs (with limited additional miniature-horse coverage under § 36.302(c)(9)) individually trained to perform work or tasks for a person with a disability. Emotional support animals are not service animals under Title III but may be covered under Fair Housing Act and Air Carrier Access Act regimes.

State law parallels: California Unruh Civil Rights Act ($4,000 statutory damages per offense); NYCHRL; Massachusetts PAA; and others provide stronger remedies than Title III's injunctive-only relief. The litigation pattern is well-established with thousands of resolved cases.

Reporting: DOJ § 36.502 administrative complaint; state AG and state civil-rights agency for statutory-damages states; private Title III actions for injunctive relief and fees; coordination with NAGDU, NFB, Guide Dogs for the Blind, and similar handler advocacy organizations.
full_description_simple:
A restaurant, store, hotel, hospital, or other business cannot kick you out because of your service animal. The ADA protects you.

What the law says:
- A service animal can go almost anywhere the public can go.
- Staff can only ask you TWO things:
  - Is the dog a service animal because of a disability?
  - What is the dog trained to do?
- Staff CAN'T ask for paperwork, a vest, a special ID, or to see the dog do its task.
- "No pets allowed" doesn't apply to service animals.
- Allergies are NOT a reason to deny you. The business has to accommodate both you and the person with allergies.
- They CAN'T charge you extra cleaning fees for the service animal.

NOT covered:
- Emotional support animals (ESAs) are NOT service animals. They have housing protection but not restaurant or store protection.
- Other pets.
- Animals other than dogs (some special rules for small horses but very limited).

Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- The business's corporate office.
- Your state attorney general.
- In California: file with CRD — you can get $4,000 per incident.
- In NYC: file with NYC Commission on Human Rights.
- For Uber and Lyft, see the rideshare listing.

key_dates.Status: Well-established law; ongoing individual enforcement

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: DOJ § 36.502; state AG; state civil-rights agencies (CRD for CA, NYCCHR for NYC, MA AG for Massachusetts, etc.); NAGDU, Guide Dogs for the Blind, NFB for handler advocacy; private Title III + state-law counsel for monetary-damages-supported claims.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Where to report:
- DOJ at ADA.gov (free).
- The business's corporate accessibility office (find on their website).
- Your state attorney general.
- For California, file Unruh Act complaint with CRD — $4,000 per offense statutory damages.
- For NYC, file with NYCCHR.
- National Association of Guide Dog Users (nagdu.org).
- NFB or other handler organization.
short_description: A restaurant, store, or rideshare refused to serve you with your service animal. No current class action — most cases are individual. Tell us what happened.
short_description_professional: ADA Title III service-animal access. 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(c) prohibits public accommodations from refusing service to handlers with service animals. Permissible inquiries are limited to two questions (whether the dog is required because of a disability; what work or task it's trained to perform). Allergies, fear, and "no pets" policies are NOT defenses. Statutory damages available in many states via Unruh, NYCHRL, MA PAA, etc.
short_description_simple: A restaurant, store, hotel, or other business refused to let you in with your service animal — or kicked you out. This is illegal under the ADA. Report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: rideshare-specific refusals (see rideshare-wheelchair-service-animal-denials); air travel (ACAA + 14 C.F.R. Part 382, with major 2020 service-animal rule change limiting in-cabin service animals to trained dogs); housing (FHA 42 U.S.C. § 3604 + ESA-distinct framework); workplace (Title I); educational settings (Title II/III + IDEA framework).
what_this_is_not_simple:
NOT covered here (see other listings):
- Rideshare (Uber/Lyft) refusals — see rideshare-wheelchair-service-animal-denials.
- Airline service-animal refusals — different framework (Air Carrier Access Act); fly-with-service-animal rules changed in 2020.
- Housing — emotional support animals ARE covered in housing under the Fair Housing Act; service animals also covered.
- Workplace — different framework (Title I).
- Schools — different framework (Title II for public, Title III for private; IDEA for students with IEPs).

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of Seattle filed by Reynoldson, et al. in the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington.

The lawsuit challenged Seattle's failure to install ADA-compliant curb ramps at intersections throughout the city. Wheelchair users and people with other mobility disabilities were systemically denied use of the public pedestrian network.

The case settled in 2017 with a citywide remediation commitment: Seattle agreed to install thousands of curb ramps on a multi-year schedule with priority routing and reporting obligations.

The case is in compliance phase. Reports of missing or non-compliant ramps can be filed with the city via Find It Fix It, with plaintiffs' counsel, or via DOJ complaint.
full_description_professional: Reynoldson v. City of Seattle (W.D. Wash., 2017 settlement), is a settled class action under Title II ADA addressing Seattle's curb-ramp installation and maintenance obligations. The settlement requires citywide installation of new ramps and remediation of non-compliant existing ramps on a court-supervised schedule. Compliance phase ongoing.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against the City of Seattle. It is now settled.

People who use wheelchairs sued Seattle because the city had not put curb ramps at most street corners. Without ramps, wheelchair users could not cross streets safely.

In 2017 the city agreed to settle. Seattle must install thousands of curb ramps on a schedule.

If you find a Seattle street corner with no ramp, a broken ramp, or a ramp that's too steep, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, multi-year remediation

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: Find It Fix It, plaintiffs' counsel, DOJ § 35.170, WSHRC.
no_documentation_path_simple: You can report without documentation. Seattle Find It Fix It app, plaintiffs' counsel, DOJ at ADA.gov, Washington State Human Rights Commission.
short_description: Settled 2017. Seattle committed to curb ramp installation on a schedule. Report violations.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under ADA Title II requiring City of Seattle to install and remediate curb ramps citywide on a court-supervised schedule. Compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: Settled in 2017. Seattle agreed to install thousands of curb ramps on a schedule. If you find a missing or broken ramp in Seattle, report it.

what_this_is_not_simple: NOT covered: King County unincorporated, Bellevue/Kirkland/other cities, Sound Transit, KC Metro, private property, Seattle public buildings.

- You used or tried to use Uber, Lyft, or similar.
- The driver refused you, charged you extra, or couldn't serve you.
evidence_guidance_professional:
Contemporaneous: app screenshot capture (cancellation timestamps, driver/vehicle identification); audio/video recording where lawful and safe; verbatim statement preservation; immediate in-app report submission preserving the carrier-side record.

Post-incident: parallel administrative complaints (DOJ + state AG + NFB testing program for Uber); state-law claims for statutory-damages jurisdictions; coordinate with DRA for systemic pattern documentation. Pattern evidence across multiple incidents (same user) is independently actionable.
evidence_guidance_simple:
When you have a problem:
- Screenshot the cancellation immediately.
- If the driver is in front of you and refuses, get their face/license plate on video if you safely can.
- Write down or voice memo the exact words used.
- Note the time and location.
- Submit the in-app report THE SAME DAY (Uber especially — settlement requires real-time complaint tracking).

After:
- File the DOJ complaint within a few days.

full_description:
Uber, Lyft, and other rideshare services are bound by the ADA's public-accommodation requirements. Multiple ongoing matters address common patterns:

Service animal refusals:
- An Uber driver refused you, drove away when seeing your service animal, or asked you to leave your dog behind. This is what NFB-CA v. Uber settled in 2016 — see the related listing. The settlement makes Uber permanently remove drivers who knowingly refuse, and Uber is required to track all such complaints.
- Lyft is in separate ongoing litigation on similar issues.

Wheelchair-accessible vehicle (WAV) access:
- Uber and Lyft are required to provide wheelchair-accessible service where they operate. In many cities WAV service is significantly slower, more expensive, or simply unavailable. Multiple lawsuits are pending alleging this constitutes disability discrimination — a "service desert" for wheelchair users that doesn't exist for non-disabled users.
- WAV waiting times of 30+ minutes (vs. 3-5 minutes for standard rides) are common.

Improper cleaning fees:
- Cleaning fees specifically charged for service animals are illegal.
- Cleaning fees for "mess" attributed to a service animal that didn't actually make a mess are illegal.
- Uber and Lyft have appeals processes for disputed fees, but they're inconsistent.

Other issues:
- Driver refusals related to mobility devices (wheelchairs, scooters, walkers).
- Driver demands for proof of disability.
- Drivers cancelling after seeing a service animal or mobility device.

Where to report:
- For Uber: in-app reporting (required by settlement), NFB testing program, plus DOJ.
- For Lyft: in-app reporting, plus DOJ.
- DOJ Civil Rights Division at ADA.gov.
- State attorney general (especially CA, NY, MA for statutory damages).
- Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org.
- NFB testing program (specifically for service-animal Uber refusals).
full_description_professional:
Rideshare accessibility is governed by ADA Title III for transportation services with DOT-regulation overlay where applicable (49 C.F.R. Part 37 for transportation-network-company analogs may apply in specific contexts; see also state PUC/TNC regulatory frameworks).

Active litigation and settlements:
- NFB-CA v. Uber, 3:14-cv-04086 (N.D. Cal., 2016 settlement; see related listing) established nationwide service-animal enforcement obligations: permanent platform removal for knowing refusals, complaint-tracking, NFB testing program, retired-judge monitor. Court retains jurisdiction.
- Ongoing Lyft service-animal litigation in parallel.
- WAV access: multiple suits against Uber and Lyft in major metros alleging unequal service availability for wheelchair users — Equal Rights Center actions, Crawford v. Uber-style claims, etc. Resolution patterns include WAV-supply requirements and waiting-time parity.
- Improper-fee litigation: cleaning fees specifically charged for service-animal accommodations are barred by 28 C.F.R. § 36.301(c).

Substantive obligations: equal service access under Title III; reasonable modifications under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(a); effective communication for deaf riders; service-animal access per § 36.302(c) (driver allergies, fear, religious objection NOT defenses); prohibition on disability surcharges per § 36.301(c).

State-law overlays: California Unruh Act ($4,000 statutory damages per offense, particularly leveraged in this area); NYCHRL; Massachusetts PAA; state TNC regulations may impose additional accessibility obligations.

Enforcement: in-app reporting (NFB-CA v. Uber settlement requires this); NFB testing program (specifically Uber-focused); DOJ Civil Rights Division administrative complaint; state AG civil-rights divisions; private Title III + state-law counsel for monetary-damages claims; DRA for systemic litigation.
full_description_simple:
Uber and Lyft have to give you rides if you have a service animal or use a wheelchair. They can't charge you extra.

Common problems:
- A driver sees your service animal and drives away.
- A driver says "I'm allergic" and refuses you.
- You can't get a wheelchair-accessible vehicle (WAV), or you wait 30+ minutes for one when other people wait 3 minutes.
- You get charged a cleaning fee because of your service dog (this is illegal).
- A driver asks for proof of your disability (also illegal).

There are active lawsuits about this:
- Uber had a big case (NFB-CA v. Uber) that settled in 2016. Uber drivers who knowingly refuse get banned from the platform. (See the related listing.)
- Lyft is currently being sued for similar things.
- Both companies are being sued over wheelchair-accessible vehicle service.

Where to report:
- Report through the Uber or Lyft app first (creates a record).
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- NFB testing program (for Uber service-animal cases especially).
- Your state attorney general.
- Disability Rights Advocates: dralegal.org.
- If you're in California, NY, or MA, also file with your state civil rights agency.

key_dates.Status: Active multi-track enforcement; particularly strong leverage for Uber service-animal claims via NFB settlement

full_description:
Multiple state attorneys general — led by Texas and joined by other states — are suing the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to challenge HHS's 2024 final rule implementing Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. The case is pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas.

The case name has changed during the litigation as HHS leadership has changed. It was originally Texas v. Becerra (when Xavier Becerra was HHS Secretary). It was subsequently restyled as Texas v. Kennedy (Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is the current HHS Secretary as of 2025).

What's at stake:
- HHS's 2024 Section 504 rule (89 Fed. Reg. 40066, May 9, 2024) is the first comprehensive update to the Section 504 regulations in decades.
- The rule codifies and strengthens many protections, including the Olmstead integration mandate (the principle that people with disabilities have the right to live in the most integrated setting appropriate — not forced into institutions).
- The rule also strengthens accessibility requirements for medical equipment, online accessibility, value assessments used in healthcare decisions, child welfare disability discrimination, and others.
- The state plaintiffs argue HHS exceeded its authority and the rule is arbitrary and capricious. They challenge multiple specific provisions.

What this could mean:
- If state plaintiffs win (in whole or in part), some of the rule's protections could be vacated.
- The integration mandate in particular is a major target — and is the foundation of cases like the DOJ investigation of Idaho's nursing-facility integration practices (see related listing).
- If HHS wins, the rule stays in force.

What this means for you:
- This case is tracking-only. There's no individual complaint to file as part of THIS case.
- Existing § 504 rights remain in force during the litigation.
- If you're affected by community-integration issues, see the doj-investigation-idaho-nursing listing.
- If you're affected by medical equipment accessibility, see medical-exam-table-access.
- If you want to support disability community amicus participation, organizations like NDRN, AAPD, the Bazelon Center, and state P&A entities are active in the case.
full_description_professional:
Texas v. Kennedy (formerly Texas v. Becerra), pending in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas, is a multi-state Administrative Procedure Act and constitutional challenge to HHS's 2024 final rule under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act (89 Fed. Reg. 40066, May 9, 2024). The case caption updated from "Becerra" to "Kennedy" reflects the post-2024 change in HHS Secretary.

The 2024 final rule represents the first comprehensive update to the Section 504 regulations (45 C.F.R. Part 84) in decades. Substantive provisions challenged include:
- Codification of Olmstead integration-mandate principles (45 C.F.R. § 84.76), grounded in Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999), and 28 C.F.R. § 35.130(d).
- Medical-diagnostic-equipment accessibility requirements paralleling the DOJ MDE Rule.
- Online accessibility standards.
- Value-assessment prohibitions in healthcare decision-making (addressing Quality-Adjusted Life Year and similar metrics).
- Child welfare and parental-disability protections.
- Specific provisions for substance-use disorder, HIV, and chronic-condition protections.

State plaintiffs argue HHS exceeded statutory authority under 29 U.S.C. § 794, advanced arbitrary-and-capricious rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A), and (in some pleadings) raised constitutional challenges. Particular targets include the integration mandate and the value-assessment provisions.

The case is pending; preliminary injunction motions and stays have been litigated. Disability-community amicus participation includes NDRN, AAPD, Bazelon Center for Mental Health Law, the National Disability Rights Network state P&A entities, the Disability Rights Education and Defense Fund (DREDF), and others.

The 2024 rule remains in effect absent a stay. Existing § 504 protections under predecessor regulations remain in force. Tracking-only listing — no individual party-status or complaint mechanism within this proceeding. For affected individual cases: see doj-investigation-idaho-nursing (Olmstead), medical-exam-table-access (MDE), and other relevant listings.
full_description_simple:
A bunch of state attorneys general are suing the federal government. They want to weaken a rule that protects the rights of people with disabilities.

The rule:
- In 2024, the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) updated the rules for Section 504. Section 504 is a key federal disability-rights law from 1973. The update was the biggest in decades.
- It strengthens the right to live in your own home or community — not in a nursing home. (This is called the "Olmstead" principle, named after a 1999 Supreme Court case.)
- It also strengthens rules about medical equipment accessibility, online accessibility, child welfare, and more.

The lawsuit:
- States led by Texas sued HHS. The case was originally called Texas v. Becerra. It's now called Texas v. Kennedy because HHS has a new Secretary.
- The states say HHS went too far and that the rule should be thrown out.

What this could mean:
- If the states win, parts of the rule could be undone.
- The community-integration part is a big target. This is the rule that backs cases like the DOJ investigation into Idaho's use of nursing homes.
- If HHS wins, the rule stays.

What this means for you:
- This case is just something to watch. There's no complaint to file here.
- Your current Section 504 rights are still active during the case.
- If you're dealing with a nursing-home situation, see the Idaho investigation listing.
- If you're dealing with medical equipment access, see medical-exam-table-access.
- Disability rights organizations like NDRN, AAPD, and the Bazelon Center file briefs in cases like this.

what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: individual § 504 enforcement under predecessor regulations or the statute (see related listings); separate HHS rulemakings not at issue in this proceeding; ADA Title II/III enforcement (separate framework; only § 504 regulations are challenged here); state-level disability-rights frameworks (unaffected).

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the Tennessee Department of Correction (TDOC) by deaf and hard-of-hearing prisoners, settled in January 2025.

The lawsuit followed a pattern of cases against state prison systems (Virginia 2010, Kentucky 2015, Maryland 2015, Georgia 2021 — see Adams v. Kentucky and Harris v. GDOC for parallels). The case alleged TDOC failed to provide effective communication and qualified interpretation to deaf inmates: no ASL interpreters for medical, disciplinary, classification, education, or programming; no videophones; no visual emergency alerts; deaf prisoners punished for not following orders they couldn't hear; programming exclusion that lengthened sentences.

The January 2025 settlement requires TDOC to:
- Provide qualified ASL interpreters at no charge for all major events.
- Install videophones for outside communication.
- Provide visual and tactile emergency notification systems.
- Train custodial and program staff.
- Implement written policies and outreach to deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates.
- Submit to monitoring.

The case is in long-term compliance phase. If you (or a family member) is a deaf or hard-of-hearing person in Tennessee state custody and isn't getting these accommodations, that's a settlement violation. Report it.
full_description_professional:
Trivette v. Tennessee Department of Correction (M.D. Tenn., January 2025 settlement), is a settled class action under Title II ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, 29 U.S.C. § 794, addressing TDOC's systemic failure to provide effective communication and auxiliary aids to deaf and hard-of-hearing inmates. The case follows the WLC-led template established in 2010 Virginia DOC, 2015 Kentucky (Adams), 2015 Maryland (Jarboe), and 2021 Georgia (Harris v. GDOC) settlements.

Settlement obligations: qualified ASL interpreter provision under 28 C.F.R. § 35.160 for medical, disciplinary, classification, educational, vocational, religious, and emergency interactions; videophone (TTY/VRS-equivalent) deployment; visual and tactile emergency notification; staff training; written policies; outreach mechanisms; monitoring.

Compliance phase ongoing. Class counsel: Washington Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights and Urban Affairs (WLC) DC Prisoners' Project (likely involved given the multi-state template); confirm with TN P&A and Disability Rights TN for current counsel.

Reports of non-compliance may proceed via class counsel, DOJ § 35.170 administrative complaint, Tennessee Human Rights Commission, and the Tennessee Protection & Advocacy entity.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against Tennessee state prisons. It is now settled.

Deaf people in Tennessee prisons sued because the prisons did not help them understand what was happening — no sign language interpreters, no videophones to call family, no flashing lights for fire alarms.

In January 2025 the case was settled. Tennessee must now:
- Provide qualified sign language interpreters for free.
- Install videophones.
- Have visual and tactile emergency alerts.
- Train staff.

If you have a family member in Tennessee state prison who is deaf or hard-of-hearing and isn't getting these things — that's breaking the settlement. You can report it.

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against Beth Israel Medical Center (now operating as Mount Sinai Beth Israel) in New York City, filed by United Spinal Association and individual patients in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York.

The case challenged Beth Israel's failure to provide accessible exam tables, transfer assistance, and effective communication accommodations to patients with mobility disabilities and other disabilities. Patients were being examined while still in their wheelchairs (which is medically inadequate for many procedures), denied transfer assistance, or denied effective communication for deaf patients.

The October 2017 settlement requires Beth Israel to:
- Procure accessible exam tables, imaging equipment, and patient lifts at clinical sites.
- Train staff on transfer assistance and effective communication.
- Provide qualified ASL interpreters for deaf patients (no charge).
- Implement intake and accommodation-request procedures.
- Submit to monitoring.

The case is in compliance phase. As Mount Sinai Beth Israel has consolidated with Mount Sinai over time, settlement obligations may have transferred or merged with broader Mount Sinai accessibility policies. Current patients encountering barriers should report.
full_description_professional:
United Spinal Association, et al. v. Beth Israel Medical Center (S.D.N.Y., October 2017 settlement), is a settled class action under Title III ADA, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, addressing accessible-exam-equipment and effective-communication obligations at Beth Israel's NYC clinical facilities.

Settlement obligations: accessible exam-table and patient-lift procurement; staff training on transfer assistance and effective communication under 28 C.F.R. § 36.302(a) and § 36.303; qualified ASL interpreter provision for deaf patients; intake-and-accommodation-request procedures; ongoing monitoring.

The corporate restructuring of Mount Sinai Beth Israel and consolidation with the broader Mount Sinai Health System has implications for settlement implementation; current patients should verify continued application or report under broader Mount Sinai accessibility frameworks.

Compliance phase ongoing. Reports of non-compliance may proceed via DOJ administrative complaint, HHS Office for Civil Rights (Section 504/Section 1557 for healthcare entities), New York City Commission on Human Rights, and New York State Division of Human Rights.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against Beth Israel Medical Center in New York. It is now settled.

Patients in wheelchairs and patients with other disabilities sued Beth Israel because the hospital did not have accessible exam tables and would not help patients transfer. Doctors were examining patients while they were still in their wheelchairs — which is bad medical care.

In October 2017 Beth Israel agreed to settle. The hospital must:
- Buy accessible exam tables and equipment.
- Train staff to help patients transfer.
- Provide sign language interpreters for deaf patients.
- Set up a system for requesting accommodations.

If you're a Beth Israel (or Mount Sinai Beth Israel) patient and you have trouble getting accessible care, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase; impacted by Mount Sinai corporate consolidation

no_documentation_path_professional: Reports may proceed without records. Channels: DOJ Civil Rights Division; HHS OCR (§ 504, § 1557); NYCCHR; NYSDHR; United Spinal Association.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You can report without records:
- DOJ at ADA.gov.
- HHS Office for Civil Rights.
- NYC Commission on Human Rights.
- NYS Division of Human Rights.
- United Spinal Association (the original plaintiff organization).
short_description: Settled October 2017. Required accessible exam equipment and patient communication accommodations. Report violations.
short_description_professional: Settled class action under ADA Title III and Section 504 binding Beth Israel Medical Center (Mount Sinai Beth Israel) to accessible-exam-equipment procurement and effective-communication compliance. October 2017 settlement; compliance phase ongoing.
short_description_simple: Settled in October 2017. Beth Israel Medical Center in NYC was sued for not having accessible exam tables and patient communication accommodations. The hospital settled and agreed to fix this. If you're a Beth Israel patient and you're not getting accommodations, you can report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: other NYC healthcare systems (separate Title III/§ 504); non-NYC Beth Israel facilities; insurance/billing; quality-of-care claims (NY medical licensing, malpractice frameworks).

NOT covered:
- Other NYC hospitals (NYU, Mount Sinai main, NewYork-Presbyterian, etc. — separate enforcement).
- Beth Israel facilities outside NYC.
- Insurance / billing issues.
- Quality of medical care (separate framework).

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of New York filed by United Spinal Association and others in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York.

The lawsuit challenged NYC's decades-long failure to install and maintain ADA-compliant curb ramps and accessible sidewalks across all five boroughs. Wheelchair users and people with other mobility disabilities faced barriers on a citywide scale.

The case settled in 2020 with a commitment from NYC to remediate sidewalks and curb ramps citywide on a multi-year schedule with priority routing, milestones, and reporting obligations.

The case is in compliance phase. Reports of missing, broken, or non-compliant curb ramps and sidewalks can be filed via NYC 311, with plaintiffs' counsel, or via DOJ complaint.
full_description_professional: United Spinal Association, et al. v. City of New York (S.D.N.Y., 2020 settlement), settled class action under Title II ADA addressing NYC's pedestrian-rights-of-way accessibility obligations across all five boroughs. Multi-year remediation with priority routing, milestone reporting, court supervision. Compliance phase ongoing. Non-compliance reports via NYC 311, plaintiffs' counsel, DOJ Civil Rights Division (28 C.F.R. § 35.170), NYCCHR, or NYSDHR.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against New York City. It is now settled.

People who use wheelchairs sued NYC. Many sidewalks and street corners in the city had no ramps, or had ramps that were too broken to use.

In 2020 NYC agreed to settle. The city must fix sidewalks and put in curb ramps across all five boroughs (Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, the Bronx, Staten Island).

If you find a broken sidewalk or missing ramp in NYC, you can report it.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, multi-year remediation across five boroughs

defendants: Walt Disney Parks and Resorts U.S., Inc.
documentation_required_professional:
Documentation supporting individual claims or class participation:
- DAS application records, including the date, mode (in-park Guest Services vs. video-call advance registration), the disability category disclosed, and Disney's response.
- Communications with Disney customer service or accommodations staff regarding the application or denial, preserved in original form.
- Medical or treatment documentation supporting the asserted disability under 42 U.S.C. § 12102, including diagnostic records, treatment histories, and any prior accommodation determinations from other entities (schools, employers, other public accommodations).
- Records of the alternative accommodation offered (return-to-queue, wheelchair use, "Lightning Lane" purchase, etc.) and the basis for its inadequacy.
- Contemporaneous records of in-park experience, including specific rides attempted, wait times, and any medical events or substantial barriers encountered as a consequence of the inadequate accommodation.
- Park admission records (tickets, annual pass documentation, hotel stays) establishing visits during the period of the challenged policy.
documentation_required_simple:
What helps if you have it:
- Records of your DAS application — whether you applied through the in-person Guest Services desk or the video-call system Disney added in 2024.
- Email or screenshots from Disney saying whether you were approved or denied.
- Notes about the conversation with the Disney representative — what disability you mentioned, what they said, what alternative they offered.
- Receipts or photos showing you visited the park.
- Medical documentation of your disability (a doctor's note, diagnosis records).
- Notes about how the alternative they offered did or didn't work for you — long lines you couldn't do, rides you had to skip, panic attacks or medical events that happened.

You don't need all of these. Even a clear written description of what happened, when, and which park is enough to start a complaint.
eligibility:
This litigation involves multiple parallel cases. You may be involved or affected if any of the following apply:
- You or a family member has a disability — developmental (autism, ADHD, intellectual disability), physical (wheelchair use, chronic illness, GI conditions), or mental (anxiety, PTSD).
- You have visited or planned to visit Walt Disney World (Florida) or Disneyland (California) within the last several years.
- You were denied DAS or assigned a "return-to-queue" or wheelchair alternative you considered inadequate for your disability.

There is no single class yet, but if the Florida Commission on Human Relations finds reasonable cause, a much broader class action could follow. In the meantime:
- If you're in Florida and want to participate, contact Andy Dogali (Dogali Law Group, Tampa).
- If you're in California, contact disability rights organizations that have filed or are tracking parallel DAS litigation.
- You can file a complaint with the DOJ at ADA.gov regardless of state.
- For Florida residents, the Florida Commission on Human Relations is currently investigating; you can file a complaint with them as well.
eligibility_professional:
No certified class currently exists; the litigation comprises multiple individual and small-group actions. Persons potentially affected by parallel or future certification include:
- Guests with qualifying disabilities under 42 U.S.C. § 12102 (developmental, physical, mental, or chronic-illness disabilities).
- Guests who attempted to use DAS at Walt Disney World (M.D. Fla.) or Disneyland (C.D. Cal.) and were either (i) denied eligibility under the October 2024 policy revision; or (ii) offered alternative accommodations (return-to-queue, wheelchair use) that did not provide equally effective access under § 12182(b)(1)(A)(ii).
- Guests subjected to the third-party "health professional" eligibility-screening process.

Class certification would require resolution of commonality and typicality questions complicated by the disability-category-specific harm theory. The Florida Commission on Human Relations investigation may yield a reasonable-cause finding that catalyzes class certification under the Florida Civil Rights Act and Fed. R. Civ. P. 23.

Parallel enforcement avenues include DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502, state-civil-rights enforcement in Florida and California, and individual Title III actions for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees.
eligibility_simple:
You may want to look into this case if:
- You or someone in your family has a disability of any kind.
- You went to Disney World or Disneyland, or were planning to.
- Disney either said you don't qualify for DAS, or gave you a different accommodation (like "leave the line and come back" or use a wheelchair) that didn't actually help.

There isn't one big class yet. But if Florida finds Disney is breaking state law, that could change.

For now:
- Florida residents: call Andy Dogali's law firm in Tampa.
- California residents: look for disability rights groups tracking DAS lawsuits.
- Anyone: file a complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice at ADA.gov. It's free.
- Florida residents: also file with the Florida Commission on Human Relations — they're investigating right now.
evidence_guidance_professional:
For prospective documentation supporting Title III claims or administrative complaints:

Pre-visit:
- Document the DAS application process: video-call screenshots (where lawful), application timestamps, disclosed disability information, third-party screener identity if disclosed, and the disposition rendered.
- Preserve all Disney communications regarding accommodation, including any boilerplate language about alternative accommodations offered.

At visit:
- Maintain contemporaneous logs of each attraction attempt, including wait times encountered, any deferral to return-to-queue procedures, and the duration and circumstances of any medical or behavioral consequences attributable to the accommodation inadequacy.
- Document park admission with dated photographic evidence (tickets, MagicBand records, Genie+ purchases, dining reservations).
- Identify and document any Disney staff (cast members) involved in accommodation-related interactions.

Post-visit:
- Memorialize the experience in a dated written record while details remain fresh.
- File parallel administrative complaints in jurisdictions of relevance: DOJ (ADA.gov), Florida CHR (for Florida-park incidents), California CRD (for Disneyland incidents).
- Preserve all park-purchase records, including Lightning Lane purchases that may bear on alternative-accommodation inadequacy theories.
evidence_guidance_simple:
If you're planning a Disney trip and want to document the experience:

Before the trip:
- When you apply for DAS, screenshot every step. Disney now uses a video call for advance registration; record or screenshot it (where legally allowed).
- Save Disney's emails confirming your application status.
- Note the date and the name of the Disney representative.

At the park:
- If you're told a specific alternative (return-to-queue, wheelchair, etc.), write down EXACTLY what was offered.
- Document each ride: which ones you could do, which you couldn't, what waiting was like, any medical issues.
- If you have a meltdown, panic attack, or medical episode caused by the inadequate accommodation, note it.
- Photos or videos of you in the park (with dates) help establish that you visited.

After:
- Send yourself emails or texts summarizing what happened so you have timestamps.
- Save all Disney park-entry receipts.
- File complaints while it's fresh — at ADA.gov and (Florida residents) Florida Commission on Human Relations.
full_description:
Walt Disney Parks operates a program called Disability Access Service (DAS) that lets qualifying guests with disabilities skip standby lines on rides at Walt Disney World in Florida and Disneyland in California. This case is a years-long, ongoing legal fight over who qualifies and what alternatives are offered to guests who don't.

The original DAS program started in 2013 in response to abuse of an older "guest assistance card" system. Under the 2013-2024 DAS, guests with documented disabilities got advance return-times for rides instead of waiting in line. In October 2024, Disney narrowed eligibility significantly — DAS is now primarily available to guests with developmental disabilities like autism. Guests with other disabilities (mobility impairments, chronic illness, GI conditions, anxiety, sensory disabilities not on the autism spectrum, etc.) are now told to use wheelchairs, "return-to-queue" alternatives, or other accommodations Disney considers equivalent.

Tampa lawyer Andy Dogali represents about 30 families with autism in Florida courts; parallel federal litigation is pending in California against Disneyland. The plaintiffs argue:
- The eligibility-screening process (often done by third-party "health professionals") improperly second-guesses who is "disabled enough."
- The alternative accommodations (return-to-queue, wheelchair use) are not actually equivalent to the original DAS for guests with the disabilities they exclude.
- The policy disproportionately harms guests with non-developmental disabilities.

In early April 2026, mediation between a Florida plaintiff and Disney failed, and the Florida Commission on Human Relations launched a state-level investigation under the Florida Civil Rights Act. A finding of "reasonable cause" by the state could trigger fines and a green light for a much larger class action.

This case is unusual in that Disney maintains the ADA does NOT require equal accommodation across different disabilities — that providing different accommodations to different disability groups is permissible. Plaintiffs disagree. The case is expected to take years.
full_description_professional:
The Disability Access Service (DAS) litigation comprises multiple parallel proceedings against Walt Disney Parks and Resorts U.S., Inc. and Walt Disney Co. challenging the DAS program under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12181 et seq., and (in Florida) the Florida Civil Rights Act of 1992. The litigation has been ongoing since approximately 2014, with Tampa-based counsel Andy Dogali representing approximately 30 families in Florida federal court (M.D. Fla., Orlando Division, before Judge Anne Conway and subsequent panels); companion federal litigation is pending in California against Disneyland.

The October 2024 policy revision narrowed DAS eligibility to guests with "developmental disabilities like autism," replacing accommodation for many other disability categories with "return-to-queue" alternatives, mandatory wheelchair use, or other Disney-designated modifications. Plaintiffs allege three principal Title III theories: (i) the eligibility-screening process — administered in part by third-party contracted "health professionals" — constitutes an impermissible disability-status determination not authorized by the regulations; (ii) the alternative accommodations are not equally effective under the "equal opportunity" standard of 42 U.S.C. § 12182(b)(1)(A)(ii); and (iii) the policy operates with disparate impact against guests with non-developmental disabilities.

Disney's defense — articulated in its California court filings — invokes the principle that the ADA does not require identical treatment across disabilities, citing the wheelchair-priority vs. captioned-seating analogy. Plaintiffs counter that this principle does not authorize categorical exclusion of certain disability classes from a program available to others similarly situated as to the relevant access barrier (line-waiting capacity).

In April 2026, mediation between a Florida plaintiff and Disney failed; the Florida Commission on Human Relations opened a state-level investigation under the Florida Civil Rights Act. A "reasonable cause" finding could trigger administrative fines and enable a much larger class action, transforming the dispute from individualized litigation into systemic enforcement.
full_description_simple:
This is a long-running fight about who gets help at Disney World and Disneyland.

Disney has a program called DAS (Disability Access Service). It lets guests with disabilities skip the long lines on rides. People with autism use it a lot, because waiting in long crowded lines is very hard for them.

In 2024 Disney changed the rules. They said DAS is now mostly for people with autism or similar conditions. Other people with disabilities — like wheelchair users, people with chronic pain, people who get sick if they stand for a long time, people who panic in crowds — were told to use a wheelchair instead, or to "leave the line and come back," or other things Disney offered.

A lot of families said: this is unfair. The new rules don't work for our disabilities. A lawyer in Tampa named Andy Dogali is representing about 30 Florida families suing Disney. There is another lawsuit in California.

In April 2026 the state of Florida also started an investigation. Mediation failed. If Florida finds Disney is breaking state law, it could mean big fines and a much bigger lawsuit.

Disney says: the law doesn't require us to treat every disability the same way. The families and their lawyers say: yes it does. This will take years.

key_dates.Status: Ongoing; potential class certification pending state investigation outcome

no_documentation_path_professional:
Documentary records are not a prerequisite to participation. The litigation is ongoing and incident reports continue to inform class development.

Avenues for class members and affected guests without contemporaneous documentation:
- DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502 (ADA.gov); DOJ has independent enforcement authority and may pursue investigation regardless of private litigation status.
- Florida Commission on Human Relations complaints (for Florida-park incidents) under the Florida Civil Rights Act, particularly given the active state investigation as of April 2026.
- California Department of Fair Employment and Housing (now Civil Rights Department) complaints under the Unruh Act for Disneyland incidents.
- Direct contact with class counsel (Dogali Law Group, Tampa) for Florida-park claims; counsel actively building the plaintiff group.
- Prospective documentation: future Disney visits should include contemporaneous records of the DAS application process, Disney's response, the accommodation offered, and the actual in-park experience.
no_documentation_path_simple:
You don't need a lot to report a problem. Even if you don't have receipts or screenshots:

- If you remember roughly when and which park (Disney World or Disneyland) and what happened, that's enough to start.
- File a free complaint with the U.S. Department of Justice at ADA.gov.
- For Florida residents, file with the Florida Commission on Human Relations — they're actively investigating Disney right now.
- Contact Andy Dogali's law firm in Tampa if you want to be part of the Florida lawsuit.

If you have a future Disney trip planned:
- Document everything. Take notes, screenshot Disney's emails, write down what happens at Guest Services.
- The more current data plaintiffs have, the stronger the case gets.
short_description: Ongoing multi-plaintiff ADA Title III litigation against Walt Disney Parks over its Disability Access Service (DAS) program. Plaintiffs allege Disney's post-2013 DAS policy and a 2024 narrowing (limiting DAS to developmental disabilities like autism) discriminate against guests with other disabilities. Florida Commission on Human Relations opened a state investigation in 2026 after mediation failed.
short_description_professional: Multi-plaintiff Title III litigation against Walt Disney Parks challenging the Disability Access Service (DAS) program, including the October 2024 policy narrowing limiting DAS eligibility to guests with developmental disabilities. Plaintiffs allege the eligibility-screening process and alternative accommodations (return-to-queue, wheelchair use) constitute disability discrimination under Title III and the Florida Civil Rights Act. Companion California federal action pending; Florida Commission on Human Relations opened state-level investigation April 2026 following failed mediation.
short_description_simple: Disney changed its disability access policy at its parks. It now mostly serves autistic guests. People with other disabilities are suing. The case has been going for years and a state investigation just started in 2026.
what_this_is_not_professional:
Scope limitations of this action:
- The litigation is limited to Walt Disney Parks and Resorts U.S., Inc. and related Walt Disney Co. entities. Universal, SeaWorld (currently subject to separate DOJ litigation regarding wheeled walkers, M.D. Fla.), Cedar Fair, Six Flags, and other theme park operators require separate enforcement.
- Pleaded claims focus on the DAS line-skipping accommodation. Other Title III barriers at Disney parks — physical-access barriers under 28 C.F.R. § 36.406 and ADA Standards, communication-access failures under § 36.303, service-animal denials under § 36.302(c), restroom accessibility — are not within the pleaded scope of these actions.
- Geographic scope is principally Walt Disney World (M.D. Fla.) and Disneyland (C.D. Cal.). Disney parks abroad (Tokyo, Paris, Hong Kong, Shanghai) are outside U.S. ADA jurisdiction.
- The Walt Disney Cruise Line, Disney retail (Disney Stores), Disney+ streaming, ESPN, and Disney Resort hotels are governed by separate Title III or Title IV (telecommunications) frameworks and are not within this litigation's scope.
- Title III provides for injunctive relief and attorneys' fees, not compensatory damages. Statutory damages may be available under California Unruh Act for Disneyland claims, but not under the Florida Civil Rights Act in the same direct-action form.

For barriers outside this case's scope, parallel enforcement avenues include DOJ administrative complaints under 28 C.F.R. § 36.502, state civil-rights agency complaints, and individual private actions for injunctive relief and fees.
what_this_is_not_simple:
A few things this case does NOT cover:
- Theme parks other than Disney. SeaWorld, Universal, Six Flags — none of those are part of this. (SeaWorld has its OWN separate DOJ lawsuit about wheeled walkers.)
- Physical accessibility at Disney parks — things like missing ramps, restroom barriers, broken elevators. Those could be ADA issues but aren't this case.
- Disney cruise line, Disney Stores, Disney+, ESPN. Those aren't part of this lawsuit.
- Disney Resorts hotels (separately may have their own issues).
- Money damages. Title III generally only gets you a court order to fix things, plus lawyers' fees. Cash for individual guests isn't the focus.

If your accessibility issue is at a different theme park or a non-park Disney thing, Ada can help you find the right path.

eligibility:

You can report a problem if:

- You encountered a barrier on an LA city-maintained sidewalk or curb ramp.

evidence_guidance_professional: Document with photographic evidence, precise location, and impact narrative. File via MyLA311 to establish city notice. Track repeat reports of the same intersection — sustained non-responsiveness is independently actionable under the settlement.

full_description:
This is a settled class-action lawsuit against the City of Los Angeles, filed by Willits, et al., a group of LA residents with mobility disabilities, in 2010 in the U.S. District Court for the Central District of California (2:10-cv-05782).

The lawsuit alleged that LA's sidewalk infrastructure had decades of deferred maintenance — cracked, lifted, broken, and missing sections, plus missing or non-compliant curb ramps — that made enormous portions of the public pedestrian network unusable for wheelchair users, scooter users, and others with mobility disabilities.

In 2015, the case settled with a landmark $1.4 BILLION commitment from LA — the largest sidewalk-accessibility settlement in U.S. history. The city agreed to spend the money over 30 years, with priority routes, milestones, and oversight. The settlement also included immediate-fix obligations for highest-priority barriers.

The case is in long-term compliance phase. Reports of inaccessible sidewalks, broken curb ramps, or improperly maintained pedestrian rights-of-way can be filed with the city or with plaintiffs' counsel.
full_description_professional:
Willits, et al. v. City of Los Angeles, 2:10-cv-05782 (C.D. Cal., filed 2010), is a settled class action under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12131 et seq., resolving systemic claims regarding the City's pedestrian-rights-of-way accessibility deficiencies.

The 2015 settlement requires the City to expend $1.4 billion over 30 years on sidewalk and curb-ramp remediation, with priority routing, milestone reporting, and ongoing court supervision. The settlement is widely cited as the largest municipal-accessibility settlement in U.S. history and is precedent for parallel actions against other major cities (Seattle, NYC, Philadelphia).

The matter remains in compliance phase. Reporting non-compliance can be made via the city's 311 system, to plaintiffs' counsel, or via DOJ administrative complaint under 28 C.F.R. § 35.170.
full_description_simple:
This was a lawsuit against the City of Los Angeles. It is now settled.

A group of LA residents who use wheelchairs sued the city in 2010. They said LA's sidewalks were so broken that wheelchair users could not use them. Many corners had no ramps at all.

In 2015 the city agreed to settle. LA must spend $1.4 BILLION dollars — over 30 years — fixing sidewalks and putting in curb ramps.

If you live in or visit LA and you find:
- A sidewalk that's too broken to roll on
- A street corner with no ramp
- A ramp that's too steep or broken

— you can report it. The city is supposed to be fixing these.

key_dates.Status: Compliance phase, multi-decade implementation

no_documentation_path_professional: Reporting may proceed without contemporaneous documentation. Channels: LA 311, plaintiffs' counsel, DOJ administrative complaint (ADA.gov), and CA Civil Rights Department for parallel Unruh/state-law claims.
no_documentation_path_simple:
Even without documentation, you can report. Just describe what you found, where, and when.

- Call LA 311 or use the MyLA311 app.
- Email plaintiffs' counsel.

short_description_simple: Settled in 2015. LA agreed to spend $1.4 billion fixing sidewalks and curb ramps over 30 years. If you find a broken sidewalk or missing ramp in LA, report it.
what_this_is_not_professional: Outside scope: County of LA unincorporated areas, LA Metro, Caltrans-maintained corridors, other municipalities, private property, City facilities beyond pedestrian rights-of-way.
